name: Eothraim
description: Superb equestrian warriors, semi-nomadic herders, and hunters who move between seasonal pastures and maintain a disunified but highly honorable society.

description_summary: Semi-nomadic horse-people — superb riders, herders and hunters who follow their flocks between seasonal pastures. Proud and honour-bound, though split into many independent groups. Toward travellers: wary but hospitable to those who show respect; quick to take offence at insult or trespass.

name: Elven Horses
description: The Horses of the Elves, like the Mearas, are directly descended from Nahar, the steed of Oromë. Nahar is immortal; his descendants are not, but Elvish Horses live to great age (100 years is not unusual). They rarely have white markings except for an occasional white "star"; large white patches (painted/piebald/skewbald) are never seen, though all other colors of lesser Horses occur, with white and silvery dappled gray more common. They prefer to live in wooded areas, as the Elves do, rather than the plains home to most Horses. More intelligent than most Horses, they need no bit or bridle, being empathetic enough to receive directions from their riders; most Elves do without a saddle. Finally, Elven Horses stand staunchly in the face of dangers that would terrify most Horses, brave even before supernatural terrors such as Trolls and the Undead. Famous individuals include Nahar (Steed of Oromë), Nimros (Steed of Glorfindel), and Rochallor (Steed of Fingolfin).
description_summary: Rare, long-lived horses bred by the Elves — more intelligent and empathetic than common steeds, needing neither bit nor bridle and answering to their rider's mind. Usually grey, silver or dappled, favouring woodland over open plain. Toward travellers: gentle and loyal to those they trust, but they will not suffer a cruel or unworthy rider.

name: Grass Cats
description: Handsome, long-legged hunter of the plains of Calenardhon, now quite rare since Men have killed or domesticated most of its prey and hunt it for fear of losing sheep, goats or kine. Its thick buff fur absorbs grass pollen in summer, turning an eerie yellow-green. It hunts by leaping onto prey's back and driving its claws into throat and neck, and has killed Men this way; in heat or combat it gives off terrifying high-pitched shrieks on winter nights.
description_summary: A large, long-legged wild cat of the open plains, now rare; its thick buff fur tinges an eerie yellow-green in summer. Hunts by springing onto its prey's back and tearing at the throat — and has killed men this way — and shrieks unsettlingly on winter nights. Toward travellers: shuns people, but deadly if cornered or driven by hunger.

name: Silvan Elves (Wood-elves)
description: When the Eldar departed from the original Elven homeland during the Elder Days, a number of their brethren remained behind. They decided not to seek the light of the Aman and were labeled as the Avari (Q. "Unwilling, Refusers"). These kindreds were left to fend for themselves during the days when Morgoth's Shadow swept over the East. During these dark times they were forced into the secluded safety of the forests of eastern Middle-earth, where they wandered and hid from the wild Men who dominated most of the lands. They became known as the Silvan or Wood-elves. Physical Character: Most are slight of build, and all are thin by mannish standards. Ruddy of complexion, with sandy hair and blue or green eyes. Generalizations are difficult, however, since there are many kindreds and there is wide variation among them. Same endurance as for Noldor Elves. Males average 6'0", females 5'9". Same lifespan as for Noldor Elves. Cannot become sick or scarred and are virtually immune to disease. -10 bonus versus cold attacks. Their vision is the same as that of Noldor Elves. The Silvan folk are superb musicians and have tremendous hearing, even for Elves. +10 to Bardic spell attacks. They are also elusive. +10 for trickery and Stalking/Hiding maneuvers. The culture of the Silvan Elves is best characterized as unstructured and rustic by Elven standards, but rich and relatively advanced when compared to the ways of Men. They have always been independent, but as of late many have settled in kingdoms ruled by the Noldor or Sindar. They are met as the common folk of the woodland realms — hunters, foresters and bowmen of Mirkwood, Lorien and the eastern eaves — wary of strangers and seldom straying far from the trees.
description_summary: Forest-dwelling Elves who never crossed the Sea, living hidden in the deep woods of the East. Slight and lithe, ruddy-skinned with sandy hair, secretive and deeply bound to their woodland realms. Toward travellers: unseen until they choose to be seen; suspicious of strangers in their woods, but not hostile without cause.

name: Hounds
description: In Middle-earth the term Hound (Q. Huo, pl. Huron) almost always means the fierce Wolfhound, ranging from the diminutive dogs of Hobbit farmers near the Shire to the great noble beasts the Men of old used to hunt Wargs and the werewolves of Morgoth. Found wherever Men or Hobbits dwell, they sometimes run wild in deserted lands. The most famous was Huan of Valinor, one of the immortal hounds of Orome, who followed Celegorm into exile, aided Luthien and Beren, and both slew and was slain by Carcharoth.
description_summary: Domesticated dogs of Men and Hobbits — here usually the fierce wolfhound, from small farm dogs to great hunting beasts once bred to bring down wolves. Found wherever folk dwell, sometimes gone wild in empty lands. Toward travellers: loyal guardians to their masters, but a wild or guard pack will challenge and harry an intruder.

name: Losandamundar
description: The rare white, long-haired Losandamunda (Q. "Snow Elephant") of the Far North is related to the Andamunda of southern Endor. These furry behemoths, though, are rarely domesticated. Instead, they are worshipped (notably by the Uax, but also by the Myri and Angcla tribes) or hunted (notably by the eastern Lossoth and Lotan).
description_summary: Rare white, shaggy-furred elephants of the frozen North — huge, cold-adapted behemoths, seldom tamed; the northern tribes either worship or hunt them. Toward travellers: peaceable grazers unless threatened, when their sheer bulk makes them deadly.

name: Sindar (Grey-elves)
description: The Sindar or "Grey-elves" are Eldar and were originally part of the great kindred called the Teleri. Unlike the Noldor, Vanyar, and bulk of the Teleri, the Sindar chose not to cross over the sea to Aman; instead they stayed in Middle-earth. They, like the Silvan Elves, are part of the Moriquendi, those Elves who never saw the Light of Valinor. Physical Character: Thin when compared to Men, the Sindar are nearly as tall as the Noldor but are generally slighter of build. They are more muscular than the Silvan folk. Most have fair hair and pale blue or grey eyes. Like the Noldor, they have light skin. Same endurance as Noldor Elves. Males average 6'5", females 6'1". Same lifespan as Noldor Elves. Cannot become sick or scarred and are virtually immune to disease. +15 bonus versus cold attacks. Vision same as that of Noldor Elves. Less musical than the Vanyar or Silvan Elves, and are less skilled in forging or crafts than the Noldor. Nonetheless, they are adept at all the arts and excel in their special provinces. No race builds better boats or ships. The Sindar are the most open and cooperative of Middle-earth's Elves. They are great teachers and borrowers and have an interest in the works of all races. Scattered through the eastern woods and the realms of their Silvan kin, they are encountered as counsellors, march-wardens and lords among the Wood-elves of Mirkwood, Lorien and the Vale of Anduin.
description_summary: Noble Grey-elves who stayed in Middle-earth rather than sail West — tall, fair-haired and pale-eyed, ancient and learned, carrying themselves with quiet authority. Toward travellers: courteous but reserved, measuring a stranger before they trust.

name: Blue Otters
description: Larger than common otters, with flattened, sharp-crested tails. Travel in small daytime groups feeding on fish eggs, small aquatic mammals and birds; den in sandbars or riverbanks, bearing only 1-2 young a year. Their large profile and valuable pelts make them endangered; protected by hunting bans across northwestern Endor, notably Gondor.
description_summary: Large river otters with flattened, sharp-crested tails, moving in small daytime family groups along rivers and denning in banks and sandbars. Prized for their pelts and now protected and scarce. Toward travellers: shy and water-bound — little threat to people.

name: Leech
description: While lauded for its medicinal qualities in some cultures, the Leech is only considered a nuisance in the delta. These nasty, slug-like creatures fasten to anything warm-blooded and use their serrated tongue to rasp through to a blood vessel. Then they remain fixed to their prey, gorging themselves until they are so full they slip off.
description_summary: Slug-like blood-sucking creatures of still, marshy water that fasten onto any warm-blooded body, rasp through the skin and gorge until full. A foul nuisance more than a danger. Toward travellers: a hazard of wading or camping by stagnant water, latching on unnoticed.

name: Andamundar
description: The Andamunda (Q. "Elephant"; pl. "Andamundar"; lit. "Long-snouted") is the smaller and more numerous cousin of the famous Mûmak. Like all its cousins, it has thick, pillarlike legs and flat, padded feet; loose skin and large ears provide heat regulation, enabling it to adapt to a variety of ecosystems. Its most notable feature is its trunk: an elongated nose and upper lip that serves as both a sensory organ and a prehensile grasping tool, used to smell, gather food, drink, and as a weapon in combat. Its pair of long tusks serve primarily as digging tools (it subsists on roots, shoots, twigs, fruit, leaves), a typical Andamunda eating about 400 pounds of food a day. Always active except for rest periods at night and a midday break, it roams in a constant search for security and food; its plodding belies its strength as a swift, agile runner that can run down and gore a foe. Unlike the Mûmak, it has a slightly humped back and one (vs. two or three) fingerlike extension at the base of its trunk, plus a gland to secrete scented waste. Like Mûmakil, females travel in herds led by an elder matriarch, bearing one offspring (after 21 months gestation) and raising the calf for 24-30 months. Domesticated by various cultures, used less than Mûmakil as riding animals but much easier to tame.
description_summary: The smaller, common cousin of the war-mûmak — a true elephant with pillar legs, great ears, a grasping trunk and long tusks, eating hundreds of pounds of foliage a day. Toward travellers: a placid browser, but a frightened or angered one is immensely dangerous; some peoples ride or work them.

name: Cave Bears
description: Huge bears (Q. "Meglirondor") weighing up to 1,400 lbs, with rough black fur striped brown on paws, ears and face. Paws can snap a neck and claws rend light metal or leather like linen, yet they are retiring creatures preferring roots, berries and fish. Monogamous and family-minded; males travel alone while females keep their cubs until grown.

description_summary: Enormous black bears up to 1,400 lbs, marked brown on paws, ears and face, with claws that rend metal and leather — yet shy creatures that prefer roots, berries and fish, and keep close family bonds. Toward travellers: avoid people, but catastrophic if surprised, cornered, or near their cubs.

name: Giant Martens
description: Huge relative of the common weasel (S. "Carlûmon", pl. "Cerlymyn"), a fierce, irrational and hyperactive creature that must eat constantly. Totally carnivorous (foraging nuts and berries only when starving), it kills anything it can disable, even Small Bears and Wild Boars. Acrobatic and an excellent climber, it dens in hollow trees and has a keen nose and fair vision.
description_summary: A huge, weasel-like predator — fierce, restless and ravenous, killing almost anything it can overpower, even boars and small bears. An agile climber that dens in hollow trees, with a keen nose. Toward travellers: won't seek out people, but is reckless and aggressive and dangerous if it does.

name: Chetmíg
description: Huge, rare grey cat (pl. "Chetmíg") with faint tabby markings for camouflage. A nocturnal predator with massive 5-7 inch forequarter fangs; its body tapers to strong, compact hindquarters and it uses its long tail to balance prodigious leaps (up to 150 ft horizontally, 40 ft vertically). Found in the northern hills and mountains.
description_summary: A huge, rare grey cat with faint camouflaging stripes and enormous 5-7 inch fangs, hunting by night in northern hills and mountains; a tapered, powerful body that makes prodigious leaps. Toward travellers: a stealthy ambush hunter of the high country, rarely seen until it strikes.

name: Madratines
description: Small, cat-like fox of the highlands of Eriador, its white mask and ears standing out sharply against reddish-brown fur. Very shy and nocturnal, it preys on rodents, birds, insects and lizards. Pairs mate for life and generally keep to a single burrow over the course of a year.
description_summary: A small, cat-like highland fox with a striking white mask and ears against reddish-brown fur. Very shy and nocturnal, preying on rodents, birds and lizards; pairs mate for life. Toward travellers: harmless and elusive — far more often heard or glimpsed than met.

name: Sloth Bear
description: Dark-brown Sloth Bear (Q. Meglituika), native to northwestern Harad and especially prevalent around Umbar, the largest bear in Middle-earth at 2,500-4,000 lbs. A huge, slow, stupid beast like a cross between a bear and a rainforest sloth, it uses formidable claws to dig vast tunnel networks, even through crumbling rock, large enough for Men to live in. Little-fearing and hard to kill thanks to its bulk and unusually thick skull, it eats whatever it can claw in the mass it needs to sustain itself.
description_summary: The largest bear in Middle-earth — a vast, slow, dim brute of the southern lands near Umbar, up to 4,000 lbs, that digs huge tunnel networks with its claws, even through rock, and is hard to kill thanks to its bulk and thick skull. Toward travellers: slow and incurious, but near-unstoppable if roused.

name: Common Men
description: A broad label for the Common Men of Eriador and Gondor: the rural and urban folk of mixed stock who make up the bulk of the ordinary population, including farmers, herders, townsfolk, traders and the rootless roles of road and frontier. They are not a single lineage but the everyday people among whom the older Edain, Dunedain and Dunnish bloodlines have long mingled.

description_summary: The ordinary folk of the settled lands — farmers, herders, townspeople, traders and drifters of mixed ancestry who make up most of the population. No single people, but the everyday humanity of road and field. Toward travellers: reactions range from open hospitality to wary suspicion with the times and the place.

name: Cathfiar
description: A fierce wild cat of the northern wilds, larger and bolder than a common wildcat, hunting alone through forest and fell. Quick and savage when cornered, it preys on hares, fowl and young deer.
description_summary: A fierce wild cat of the northern wilds, larger and bolder than a common wildcat, hunting alone through forest and fell for hares, fowl and young deer. Toward travellers: keeps away from people, but quick and savage if cornered.

name: Orc Elite Guards
description: Picked Orc warriors, bigger and better-armed than the common run, set to guard a captain, a fortress gate or some matter of the Enemy's; Sauron's own guards among them are grimly disciplined and far more dangerous than ordinary goblins.
description_summary: Picked orc warriors — bigger, better-armed and grimly disciplined — set to guard a captain, a fortress gate or some business of the Enemy, far more dangerous than common goblins. Toward travellers: hostile and deadly; they kill or capture intruders without parley.

name: Orc Slaves & Guards
description: A column of wretched thralls — Men, lesser Orcs and other captives — driven to labor under the lash of Orc slave-drivers. The guards are brutal and watchful; the slaves, broken and desperate, may turn on their masters given the smallest chance.
description_summary: A column of wretched captives — men, lesser orcs and other thralls — driven to labour under the whips of brutal orc slave-drivers. Toward travellers: the guards are violent and watchful; the broken slaves may beg for help, or turn on their masters given any chance.

name: Elven Noble
description: A high-born Elf of one of the great houses, travelling with attendants between the Elven realms on errands of state, kinship or lore. Met in Lindon, the Ered Luin and the eastern woods alike, such a lord or lady bears authority lightly but unmistakably.
description_summary: A high-born Elf of one of the great houses, travelling with attendants on errands of state, kinship or lore, bearing authority lightly but unmistakably. Toward travellers: gracious and dignified but remote; a meeting feels like an audience as much as an encounter.

name: Common Folk
description: Ordinary working people of town and countryside — farmers, herders, craftsfolk, labourers and their families going about daily life. Met wherever folk dwell, they want chiefly to be left in peace, and their welcome to strangers ranges from open hospitality to wary suspicion depending on the times and the road.

name: Foxes
description: Wild foxes of wood, hill and field — sharp-witted solitary hunters of rabbits, fowl, mice and carrion. Bold around farms and middens, they slip away at the first sign of danger and are more often known by their tracks and raided hen-roosts than by sight.

name: Rabbits
description: Wild rabbits and hares of field, down and woodland edge — quick, prolific and ever-watchful. A staple of hunters, hawks and foxes, they bolt for cover at the least alarm.

name: Horse Herds
description: Herds of horses bred and grazed on the open grasslands, prized for riding, war and trade. Watched by mounted herders, the finest stock is jealously guarded and ranks among the region's most valued exports.
description_summary: Herds of horses grazed on open grassland, prized for riding, war and trade and watched over by mounted herders. Toward travellers: the finest stock is jealously guarded, and to be caught near it uninvited invites suspicion.

name: Sheep Pastures
description: Flocks of sheep grazing the hills, downs and rough pasture for wool, milk and meat. Shepherds and their dogs work the high ground through the seasons, and the wool-clip is a cornerstone of local trade.
description_summary: Flocks of sheep on hills, downs and rough pasture, worked for wool, milk and meat by shepherds and their dogs. Toward travellers: a sign of peaceful, settled uplands; the wool-clip is a cornerstone of local trade.

name: Apiaries
description: Kept hives and wild bee-trees worked for honey and wax — a sweet and valuable harvest where sugar is unknown. Bee-keepers tend their skeps in meadow and orchard, and a good honey-year is cause for celebration.

name: Fisheries
description: Waters worked for their fish — rivers, lakes and coasts where folk net, trap and line their catch. Drying-racks, weirs and fishing-boats mark the settlements, and salt or smoked fish keeps the region fed through winter.
description_summary: Rivers, lakes and coasts worked for fish, marked by drying-racks, weirs and boats, with salt and smoked fish keeping the region fed through winter. Toward travellers: a dependable source of food and a sign of settled waterside folk.

name: Timberlands
description: Worked woodlands felled for timber and firewood, the logs hauled and floated downriver to mill and market. Woodcutters' camps and sawpits dot the forest edge, and good timber is a steady source of wealth.
description_summary: Worked woodlands felled for timber and firewood, the logs floated downriver to mill and market, with woodcutters' camps and sawpits along the forest edge. Toward travellers: a place of industry and rough company rather than wilderness.

name: Lamprey
description: A cylindrical, grey, jawless fish, the Lamprey is a parasite that attaches itself to other creatures, like a leech with a circular maw. It then remains in place, slowly burrowing into the flesh and swallowing blood until sated. (A wound from a Lamprey will bleed at a rate of two hits/round for each round it was in place.)
description_summary: A jawless, eel-like fish with a round, rasping sucker-mouth — a water parasite that latches onto larger creatures and bores in to feed on blood until full. Toward travellers: a foul hazard of swimming or fording, fastening on unseen and leaving a slow-bleeding wound.

name: Morgai Flies
description: Vicious, biting, parasitic insects, Morgai Flies are one of the nasty nuisances of life in Mordor. They can found all over the Black Land, particularly in Gorgoroth, where they are known as Orcflies. Their sole food is blood, but enjoy feeding off nearly any beast they can find. Relatives of common Horseflies, Morgai Flies are large and dun, gray, or black in color, with red eyes and a red blotch, like Sauron's Lidless Eye, on their backs. Many chroniclers believe them to be monsters of the Dark Lord's design. This may be true of some of the larger subgroups, but those of the basic variety are simply huge, primeval vermin. Morgai Flies carry virtually all bloodborne diseases, and they played a major part in spreading the Great Plague of T.A. 1636-1637.
description_summary: Vicious blood-sucking flies that swarm the wastes of Mordor, where Orcs call them "Orcflies" — large and dun-grey with red eyes and a red mark on the back like a watching eye, rumoured to be the Dark Lord's own breeding. Toward travellers: a maddening, biting torment to any beast or person crossing their lands.

name: Crakes And Coots
description: A black-and-purple ducklike species, the Crake is known as a noisy and obnoxious waterbird. It has wide, splayed feet, allowing it to dash across pondweed and mudflats. Crakes are also excellent swimmers and remarkably swift flyers. They are among the most flexible and adaptable species in all of Middle-earth, ranging as far north as Forodwaith, Mur, and Urb. The Crake closest relative, the Coot, builds island nests up to three or four feet across from reeds and weeds.
description_summary: Noisy black-and-purple ducklike water-birds with broad splayed feet that let them dash across mud and pond-weed; strong swimmers and swift flyers, hardy and widespread. Toward travellers: harmless, but their racket can give away a quiet camp by the water.

name: Grass Grouses
description: A plump, gallinaceous (chickenlike) bird of the grasslands of Calenardhon (Rohan), the tasty Grass Grouse is the preferred prey of many hunters, be they winged or two- or four-legged. Many a man, Red Fox, or Hawk will pass easier prey by for a crack at a greasy Grass Grouse. Their black-speckled brown plumage provides good camouflage in the tall grasses, so the birds prosper and spread in spite of their inherent appeal to the palate. The male booming mating cry is most reminiscent of a drunk blowing over the neck of a bottle.
description_summary: Plump, chicken-like ground-birds of the grasslands, brown speckled black for camouflage — a favourite, tasty quarry of hunters, foxes and hawks; the male's mating call booms like someone blowing across a bottle. Toward travellers: harmless, chiefly welcome as game.

name: Jatewoones
description: The Jatewoone is also known as the cleaverbeak or marsh bird. Standing 9 feet tall and endowed with Swan-like necks and long, powerful legs, these white waterfowl sport a scarlet band across the eyes. From a distance they might look like a large crane, although they are not of that family. Their beak is a light grey with the exception of the sharp, brilliant indigo dorsal ridge from which they get their name. Birds of prey in every sense, their legs end in talons that can grip even small creatures tightly, and their beaks are made for tearing. Jatewoones live on snakes, fish, and small mammals. Their reputation for hostility comes from their protection of their nests, usually hidden in swampy areas; they will also attack when surprised by noisy people, and can split a skull. They mate for life and produce one or two eggs every year or every other year; the young grow at a remarkable pace. Jatewoones can fly, but if the need to escape danger arises, it is more common to see them running at high speeds, using stillwater areas for cover.
description_summary: A huge white marsh-bird standing some nine feet tall, swan-necked and long-legged, with a scarlet band across the eyes and a hooked, indigo-ridged beak. Despite the crane-like look it is a true bird of prey, taking snakes, fish and small animals with taloned legs and tearing beak. Toward travellers: wary of people, but its size and beak make it dangerous if provoked.

name: Bitterns
description: A wading and fishing bird found in the deltas of northwestern Endor, the Bittern, or Northern Heron, is known for its strange resonant, mournful cry. It is a slow, cunning, deliberate hunter that feeds on snakes, crustaceans, and fish. Bitterns migrate, spending their winters in the wetlands along the temperate seacoasts and their summers in the lakes and deltas of the North.
description_summary: A solitary wading bird of the northern deltas — a kind of heron known for a strange, mournful, resonant cry. A slow, patient, cunning hunter of fish, snakes and crayfish that winters on temperate coasts. Toward travellers: harmless; its eerie call carries far across the marsh at dusk.

name: Neekerbreekers
description: Noisy, cricketlike insects that are found in marshy areas in the northerly regions of Endor (e.g., Arthedain), Neekerbreekers are little more than a major nuisance. Their name is derived from their noisy, incessant night cry. Those experienced with and accustomed to travel in these regions soon learn to tolerate the sound of Neekerbreekers, but travellers carrying dried grains, flour, or waybread are well advised to keep these supplies tightly wrapped in areas where the hideous "neek-breek, neek-breek!" can be heard, since Neekerbreekers have been known to infest and devour such food. Neekerbreekers are found in particularly foul swamps. Gigantic relatives of the mosquito, these carnivores prefer solid meat. So, while it does not suck blood, a Neekerbreeker will appropriate small chunks of a man's flesh. Any wound inflicted by a Neekerbreeker has a chance of being infected with Yellow Rheum, a 0 level illness.
description_summary: Cricket-like marsh insects of the northern wetlands, named for their endless night-cry of "neek-breek, neek-breek." Little more than a nuisance — though they will infest and devour unguarded grain, flour or waybread. Toward travellers: a sleepless din, and a threat to loosely-wrapped food stores.

name: Kingfishers
description: The smallest of Endor's fisherfowl, the Kingfisher is also the most attractive. It has a large, silvery bill and silver, blue, orange and white feathers. The Kingfisher's riverbank home belies its beauty, though, for it is little more than a dirty hole full of old fish bones and rotting mess. Besides fish, the Kingfisher preys on insects, centipedes, and small lizards and amphibians.
description_summary: The smallest of the fishing-birds and the most beautiful — a jewel of silver, blue, orange and white perched by the water, diving for fish, insects and small lizards; its riverbank nest, though, is a foul hole of bones and rot. Toward travellers: harmless, a bright flash of colour along any river.

name: Green-Winged Crows
description: A lively, intelligent bird of the marshes and shores, the Green-winged Crow can be tamed even as an adult. They make effective messengers, able to find their way home from any direction within a 75-100 mile radius. However, like most of the Corvidae, they are attracted and distracted by shiny objects. With their shiny black plumage marked distinctively on the "shoulder" of the wing with a patch of iridescent green, these crows make attractive pets.
description_summary: A clever, lively marsh-and-shore crow, glossy black with an iridescent green wing-patch. Tameable even as adults, they make fine homing messengers, though like all their kin they are easily distracted by anything shiny. Toward travellers: harmless and curious; sometimes kept as pets or message-bearers.

name: Zurkuvi
description: The Zurku (Ap. "Trail lizard"; pl. "Zurkuvi") is found throughout Harad, particularly in the south. It is related to the Gorbet of northwestern Endor (Gorbets are especially well known in Dunland). A dull yellow color with black and red stripes on its head and neck, the Zurku is a colorful creature; it has a bright orange, arrow-shaped patch on its back. It typically grows to a length of 12" and can be found in the summer months. The lizard has no natural enemies because it tastes terrible and secretes a mild nerve toxin from its skin; the poison is absorbed through the skin and paralyzes victims for 10-20 minutes (it numbs the limbs of larger attackers for only awhile, but for most animals is an effective deterrent). What is notable about this relatively harmless lizard is its reputation as a pathfinder: it is believed by most Haradrim that if they are lost and follow a Zurku, it will lead them to a trail or road. This legend is shared by the Dunlendings and appears to have originated among the ancient Southrons during the early Second Age. There may be some truth to this belief, since the Zurku apparently understands that where there are trails there are beasts (and beast-droppings), and where there are beasts there are flies.
description_summary: A small, vividly-marked lizard of Harad — dull yellow with a black-and-red striped head and an orange arrow-shape on its back, about a foot long. It has no natural predators because it tastes vile and oozes a mild nerve-toxin from its skin that paralyses for a time. Toward travellers: not aggressive, but handling one risks a numbing paralysis from its skin.

name: Nimfiara
description: Larger and rarer than Elk, the Nimfiara (S./Du. "White Hart") is the strongest and most imposing breed of Deer found in northwestern Middle-earth. Reputedly mystical creatures, they range in the ancient woodlands of Rhovanion, Gondor, Calenardhon, and Eriador, and are found deep within the Old Forest and the Dark Wood, and a few have been spotted in Fangorn. The Nimfiara's lustrous silver pelt is prized for fine garments, so it is hunted and trapped by woodsmen. Worse, its horns are said to produce a potion which retards aging. Thus, the beast's value threatens its survival and, aside from the inaccessibility of their habitat, the Nimfiara has few defenses against extinction. They are, however, very wily, and they will put up a good fight when cornered.
description_summary: A rare, magnificent white hart — the largest and most imposing deer of the northwest, with a lustrous silver pelt, haunting ancient forests like the Old Forest and Fangorn. Reputed to be mystical; hunted for its prized pelt and for horns said to brew an age-slowing potion. Toward travellers: shy and elusive, a near-mythical glimpse rather than any threat.

name: White Foxes
description: Found throughout the temperate highlands and northern plains of Middle-earth, the White Fox is especially famous in Calenardhon (Rohan), where it is rare and prized as a hunting foe by Gondorian frontiersmen and Rohir lords. It is only white in winter; its dense pelt is normally bright silvery grey and, like the Grass Cat, picks up grass pollen to look oddly light green, while black-tipped guard hairs keep even the winter coat from being truly white. It dens in shallow burrows and eats small mammals, ground birds and their eggs, and large insects such as grasshoppers.
description_summary: A highland fox prized as quarry — silvery-grey most of the year and white only in winter (its summer coat even tinges faint green from grass pollen). Dens in shallow burrows, hunting small mammals, ground-birds, eggs and large insects. Toward travellers: harmless and elusive, a hunting foe more than any danger.

name: Thrushes
description: Thrushes are large songbirds. Nearly as intelligent and gifted as ravens, they are among Radagast the Brown's principal messengers and scouts. They are also long-time friends of wise Men in many regions, but especially in Erebor and the Dale. Dwarves admire them, but cannot communicate with them because the Thrushes speak too quickly and in too high pitched voices (one would imagine that Ents would have similar problems). The Thrush language can, however, be learned by Men and Elves who care enough to make a study of such things. Thrushes are very keen eyed and have a strong streak of curiosity in them, so they make excellent scouts. The Old Thrush of Erebor helped Thorin and Company find the secret entrance to Smaug's cave, and warned Bard the Bowman of Smaug's weak spot.
description_summary: Large, clever songbirds, nearly as wise as ravens — keen-eyed, curious, and used as messengers and scouts by Radagast and by wise folk, especially around Erebor and Dale. They speak too high and fast for Dwarves to follow. Toward travellers: harmless and inquisitive; a friend to the well-meaning and a sharp watcher of all who pass.

name: Boars
description: Fierce wild pigs, ancestors of all domesticated breeds, found throughout the temperate regions of Endor; the largest, the Red Boar, lives east of the Orocarni in the easternmost continent (particularly Aegan). Exceptionally dangerous and largely nocturnal, an alarmed Boar charges regardless of size, trampling with hooves and slashing with tusks, and injuries short of a limb hardly slow it. Hunted everywhere with stout cross-barred spears, since a speared Boar runs up the shaft at the hunter. Omnivores travelling in groups of 1-20; the famed Boar of Everholt of the Firien Wood gored King Folca of Rohan to death in T.A. 2864, boar and king dying together.

Largely nocturnal and exceptionally dangerous when roused: an alarmed boar charges anything, trampling and slashing with its tusks, and barely slows from wounds. Hunted with stout cross-barred spears, because a speared boar runs up the shaft at the hunter. Toward travellers: avoids people, but a startled or cornered boar is a deadly charge.

name: Elk
description: Large deer of the northern forests and marshes, the bulls bearing broad spreading antlers. Powerful and aggressive in the rut, an elk (or moose) can be a match for a wolf or a careless hunter.

Usually placid, but powerful and aggressive in the autumn rut, when a bull can match a wolf or gore a careless hunter. Toward travellers: leaves people alone, but rutting bulls deserve a wide berth.

name: Fish
description: The fish of river, lake and sea — from small fry to great pike, salmon and sea-fish — a staple food for folk and beasts alike. Their abundance shapes where fishers settle, and the greater predators of the deep are not to be taken lightly.

name: Giant Spiders
description: Web-spinning descendants of Ungoliant; the lesser kind haunt Mirkwood and other gloomy places, speaking Westron and Black Speech and cooperating to trap victims with paralysing then dissolving venom. Greater ones include Shelob, guardian of Cirith Ungol in Mordor, and Enna San Sarab of Dol Guldur.
description_summary: Monstrous web-spinning spiders descended from Ungoliant, lurking in gloomy forests like Mirkwood — cunning enough to speak and to cooperate in trapping prey, which they bind and poison with paralysing, flesh-dissolving venom. The greatest, like Shelob, are horrors in their own right. Toward travellers: deadly ambush predators that snare and devour the unwary in the dark.

name: Merchaithrais
description: The Shadowhorn, or "Morchaithras" (pl. "Merchaithrais" or "Morchaithrais"), is a large, flat, black Ray equipped with a long, whiplike tail. Its tail is studded with short but exceptionally sharp barbs that deliver knifelike cuts and inject victims with a powerful (3rd lvl) muscle venom. The Ray expanded pectoral fins form triangular wings that extend along its head and trunk, giving the fish a diamond shape. The tips of these wings are equipped with electricity-producing organs that can stun or immobilize a swimmer. Merchaithrais prefer to swim along the bottom of the coastal shelf, burrowing themselves in the sand near shoals in order to await their prey. While swimming, they flap their pectoral fins and move gracefully and quickly through the water, as if flying.
description_summary: A large, flat, black sting-ray — diamond-shaped with broad wing-like fins — that glides along the coastal sea-bed and buries itself in sand. Its whip-like tail bears sharp venomous barbs, and its fin-tips can deliver a stunning shock. Toward travellers: a hidden danger to anyone wading or swimming shallow coastal waters.

name: Kine Of Araw
description: The Kine of Araw are the largest of Middle-earth's bovids, that is creatures with split, even-toed hoofs. It is believed that these large, white cattle are the gift of the Vala Oromë (Araw). This is quite possibly true. The beasts are found on the shores of the Sea of Rhûn and on the Plain of Talath Harroch, east and south of Mirkwood. As far as anyone can tell, the Easterling tribes are the only people to have domesticated these creatures and used them as draft beasts, since the Rhovanion Northmen revered the Kine and used them only for breeding other cattle. Easterlings employed Kine to pull the Great Wains of their chiefs. During the brief existence of the Wainrider Kingdom in Wilderland (T.A. 1854-99), dozens of these magnificent beasts were pressed into service, and many perished in the raiding and wars that accompanied the Wain-easterlings' occupation. Many people of central Middle-earth value the long gray horns of these cattle, both to use as trumpets and drinking horns and to cut into sections for superb compound bows.
description_summary: The largest cattle in Middle-earth — great white oxen said to be a gift of the Vala Oromë, found by the Sea of Rhûn and the eastern plains. Revered by some peoples and used by the Easterlings as powerful draught-beasts to haul their great wains. Toward travellers: placid and imposing; a wonder to see, and sacred to many who herd them.

name: King Spiders
description: King Spiders are similar to but unlike the Giant Spiders of Mirkwood. They do not build large webs but rather dig deep pits, 2-3 ft wide and 8-10 ft deep. A King Spider caps this pit with a lid cleverly woven from a small amount of spider-silk, disguising the covering with leaves and other vegetation so that it is extremely hard (-30) to detect. Victims falling through the lid receive one to three +25 short sword attacks from the strong, knife-like silk tendrils. The pits are usually built on slopes. They support no more than 40 pounds of weight. The K i n g Spider, who has been waiting in a small side passage, leaps upon the victim and biles it, injecting its special poison, Kennesank. A RR f a i l u r e of 1-50 results in paralysis; a failure of 51+ results in death as the internal organs dissolve. If the victim dies, the spider will eat it. If not, the thrifty spider saves the meal. If it is a female who has recently mated, she may lay 6-12 eggs on top of the helpless prey and leave, sealing the hole more sturdily behind her. In two days, when the spiderlings hatch, they will spend three weeks living on the food left behind by their mother. Surely one of Endor's most vicious insects, the rare but often lethal Hummerhorn is the stuff of legend. Each weighs 6-8 lbs and has a razor sharp, pointed proboscis of chitin (transparent horn). It gores a victim to death, then feasts on the corpse with three sets of mandibles. They resemble gigantic black and grey wasps, and their only failing is the noise they make whenever they approach.
description_summary: A trapping spider quite unlike the web-spinners — it digs a deep pit and roofs it with a camouflaged silk lid hidden under leaves, almost impossible to spot. Anything breaking through falls onto knife-like silk spikes while the spider waits in a side tunnel. Toward travellers: a lethal hidden trap on slopes and trails; the danger is the ground itself.

name: Slirdu
description: Strange arboreal creatures (the Slird, pl. "Slirdu") found only in the forests of the remote islands off the southeast coast of Middle-earth, most in colonies of 2-40 on the isle of Vulm Shryac. Green or green-with-red-stripes, they are four- or five-armed invertebrates resembling both squids and starfish, adapted to land with true lungs and a quasi-skeleton of thick insulated nerves; each prehensile arm joins a squid-like trunk holding two eyes, a beak-like mouth and an ink sack. Nesting near treetops and moving fast through the canopy, these true carnivores eat ~20 lbs of meat a day, enveloping prey, immobilising it with suction pads and spraying toxic ink that stuns, blinds or knocks out; their rudimentary nerve-stem brain makes them little more than honed killing organisms.
description_summary: Strange tree-dwelling creatures of remote southeastern islands — four- or five-armed beasts like a cross between a squid and a starfish, green or red-striped, with a beaked mouth and an ink-sac, racing through the forest canopy. Fast carnivores that envelop and immobilise their prey. Toward travellers: alien and predatory, but found only on far-off islands few will ever reach.

name: Great Eagles
description: The Great Eagles are the most noble of all birds, having been created by the Valar Manwe, Lord of the Air, and Yavanna, Queen of the Earth. Appointed as overseers of all the Kelvar, they have played many glorious roles in the history of Middle-earth. They have rescued numerous champions in their hour of greatest need: both the Noldo Elf Maedhros, imprisoned in Thangorodrim, and Gandalf the Grey, confined at Orthanc by Saruman, owe their salvation to these graceful creatures (they also aided Gandalf after his combat with the Balrog of Moria). Great Eagles are as intelligent as any of the Free Peoples but are not numbered among them, presumably because they lack the power to build things and fashion crafts. These huge birds are not to be trifled with: fierce foes when aroused to anger, with little patience for faint-heartedness in the face of danger. They are stalwart foes of Evil and especially hate any other creature of the air who has allied with the Valar enemies.

description_summary: The noblest of all birds, made by the Valar and as wise as any of the Free Peoples — vast eagles who have borne rescue to great figures in their darkest hours and serve as far-seeing watchers of the world. Toward travellers: aloof and rarely seen, but a friend to the good in dire need; never to be commanded.

name: Vault Shrikes
description: The Vault Shrike is a tiny, bright red Bat with a long nose and tongue with which it pokes into holes and crevices for insects to eat. It also secretes from its tongue a highly toxic nerve poison that enables it to deal with larger prey. These Vault Shrikes paralyze or kill by means of the poison. It then rips its victims to shreds with its tiny sharp teeth. The Vault Shrike diet is further enhanced by its ability to catch small frogs in an ironically froglike manner: by snatching them in midleap with that long tongue. Vault Shrikes get their name from their unnerving, shrieking cry. They are only found in Moria and the caverns of the Misty Mountains.
description_summary: A tiny, bright-red bat with a long probing tongue, haunting the deeps of Moria — it licks insects from crevices, but also coats its tongue with a potent nerve-poison to paralyse larger prey, then shreds them with needle teeth; named for its eerie shrieking cry. Toward travellers: small but venomous, a swarming peril in the dark places it haunts.

name: Echo Hawks
description: The Echo Hawk is a large subterranean bird and a menace that few would expect to meet underground in a cave. It is a raptor that has totally adapted to life in a lightless environment, utilizing both infravision and echo-location for its hunting. The echo Hawk nostrils are unusually large, giving it a keenness of scent quite rare in a bird. Its beak and talons are large, hard, and sharp, so much so that the Echo Hawk can pierce through most types of armor. As is usual with the raptors, the female Hawk is larger than the male. Otherwise the sexes are similar, with dull white feathers, colorless skin, and beaks and talons like translucent horn.
description_summary: A cave-dwelling raptor fully adapted to lightless depths, hunting by echo-location, scent and night-sight; pale and near-colourless, with beak and talons hard enough to pierce most armour. Toward travellers: an unexpected, dangerous predator to meet underground, striking from the dark.

name: Flying Foxes
description: A variety of large bat, the Flying Fox makes its home amid brambles or in the tops of dense, partially-submerged trees of the coastal wetlands. They are most common in the marshes and scrublands of the East, although a few smaller varieties make their homes in Rhun and one group lives in southern Eriador. Unlike most bats, the Flying Fox thrives in daylight, having good eyesight and a refined sense of smell. Using its long snout, it hunts rodents in the scrub and bites wild berries which might elude its more flatfaced cousins. The Flying Fox is considered synonymous with evil magic in many areas of eastern Middle-earth.
description_summary: A large daylight-flying bat of coastal wetlands and eastern marshes — keen-eyed and long-snouted, hunting rodents and biting wild berries. Widely held to be an omen of evil magic. Toward travellers: harmless in itself, though feared and ill-regarded wherever it appears.

name: Locusts
description: Grasshopper-like insects that breed in dry seasons into vast devouring swarms, stripping field and pasture bare in hours. A locust-year spells famine, and the darkening cloud of their flight is dreaded by every farmer.
description_summary: Grasshopper-like insects that breed in dry years into vast devouring swarms, stripping fields and pasture bare in hours. Toward travellers: a locust-year means famine; the darkening cloud of their flight is dreaded by every farmer.

name: Pond Bats
description: A small black creature, the Pond Bat rarely attacks Men or Elves unless panicked and confused, or trapped. It is, however, a noted carrier of fleas and other parasites, which it leaves in isolated dwellings and camps. Thus, they are a common cause of Cyclic Fever.
description_summary: A small black bat that rarely troubles people unless trapped or panicked, but is a notorious carrier of fleas and parasites left behind in camps and empty dwellings — a common source of "Cyclic Fever." Toward travellers: little direct threat, but a spreader of disease in isolated shelters.

name: Hunting Bats
description: A large, carnivorous bat of the White Mountains, Hunting Bats are attracted to the smell of blood. They will pounce upon a bleeding victim, tearing flesh from bone with their small, razor-sharp teeth. In addition, they are notorious carriers of disease. Hunting Bats usually dwell near the entrance of a cave (on the inside, of course), but they can also be found in the crevices of cliffs and in abandoned buildings.
description_summary: A large carnivorous bat of the White Mountains, drawn to the smell of blood — it pounces on a bleeding victim and tears flesh with razor teeth, and spreads disease; it haunts cave-mouths, cliffs and ruins. Toward travellers: dangerous to the wounded, swarming onto anything that bleeds in its haunts.

name: Eris Squids
description: This small tentacled sea creature is found along the Mumakani coast, in the warm South Sea. It is hunted by alchemists and others who prepare magical substances for its ink. The exact use of this solution in magical preparations is not known to those outside this isolated southern region of Middle-earth. Mumakani and Kiran peoples, however, prize the substance for its ability to produce camouflage paste and invisible ink.
description_summary: A small tentacled squid of the warm southern coast, hunted for its ink, which southern alchemists prize for camouflage paste and invisible ink. Toward travellers: harmless; of interest only to those who trade in its ink.

name: Gorcrows
description: Smaller cousins of the evil Crebain, Gorcrows are not so much evil as indifferent to suffering. Not nearly as bright as their larger cousins, Gorcrows live to eat (although, if asked, they would probably insist that they merely eat to live). They are attracted to shiny things, and Gorcrows have picked up unbelievable numbers of shiny trinkets and small coins. Their untidy, communal nesting sites are often littered with minor yet still significant amounts of wealth. However, since Gorcrows will cooperate in protecting their nest sites, would-be plunderers are well-advised to be very cautious and to wear helmets with small eyeslits: Gorcrows are notoriously fond of eyeballs!

description_summary: Dull-witted cousins of the evil Crebain — not malicious, just indifferent and endlessly hungry, with a magpie's lust for shiny things; their messy communal nests are often littered with hoarded coins and trinkets. Toward travellers: harmless singly, but they band together fiercely to defend a nest, so plundering their trove is risky.

name: Ravens
description: Ravens are among the more intelligent birds to be found in Middle-earth. Not as noble nor as wise as the Great Eagles, Ravens are nevertheless on the side of good, unlike all too many of their lesser Corvidae cousins. They are reputedly the messengers of the Vala Namo (Mandos) and are associated with Fate and Prescience. The Ravens of Erebor befriended and counselled the Dwarves of the Line of Durin during the Third Age. These noble birds live a long time: Roac of Erebor lived past 150 years of age. Brilliant and somewhat enchanted, most Ravens can speak the tongues of the Free Peoples (or at least of Men and Elves; the tongue of the Dwarves is too secret and that of the Ents too lengthy for ravens to learn). Conjurers have long sought out the foreknowledge of Ravens.
description_summary: Highly intelligent, long-lived birds aligned with good — said to be messengers of the keeper of the dead and linked to fate and foresight; the Ravens of Erebor long counselled Durin's Dwarves, and most can speak the tongues of the Free Peoples. Toward travellers: wise and watchful, a possible ally and bearer of news to the worthy.

name: Cavern Wings
description: Cavern Wings are large, intelligent Bats found predominantly in Moria, although they have been reported in other caverns in the Misty Mountains. They seem to have a very sophisticated "biting order," culminating in a leader which directs the other bats in their group hunts. Their weapon is their razor sharp claws, with which they rip their victims into bite-sized shreds. Their voices are low-pitched for bats, allowing most victims some warning of their approach, although this rarely helps as the Cavern Wings are very quick and maneuverable. They have dark gray to black fur, often speckled in younger individuals; the best defense is to slay the leader, after which the rest retreat until the flock chooses a new leader.
description_summary: Large, intelligent bats of Moria and the Misty Mountains that hunt in directed packs under a dominant leader, slashing prey to shreds with razor claws; their low calls give a little warning, though they are too quick for it to help. Toward travellers: a coordinated, deadly swarm in the dark of caverns.

name: Carnantor
description: Huge ocean-dwelling Eels, the Carnantor (Q. "Red Maws"; sing. "Carnanto") are among the most dangerous of water beasts. They reside in the reefs in the eastern portions of the South Sea (S. "Haragaer") and the southern reaches of the East Sea (Q. "Romenear"), favoring the ample shelter, rich food sources, and warm waters off Endor southeastern coast. These seemingly ever-hungry denizens of the undersea shelf subsist on sharks and other large fish, battling Kraken, Whales, and Fell Turtles for control of the waters. Carnantor are particularly dominant and dangerous in the spring, when the Eels return to their spawning grounds off the coasts of Waw, Cevra, Arth, and Vulm Shryac, crowding the reefs and feeding and mating in an awe-inspiring frenzy. The male is up to 12 feet long, while the female reaches lengths of 45 feet.
description_summary: Huge ocean eels of warm southern reefs — among the most dangerous of sea-beasts, ever-hungry, preying on sharks and battling kraken and whales for the deep waters, fiercest in spring. Toward travellers: a grave peril to any boat or swimmer in their reef-waters.

name: Vereut Eagles
description: The Vereut Eagle inhabits the hills of eastern Eriador. It is black over most of its body and wings, but its hue reaches a dark purple near the face and wing tips. The Eagle beak and eyes stand in stark contrast, for they both are a bright yellow color. When hunting, they fly silently, low to the ground, enabling them to skirt the tops of hills and ridges to surprise their favorite prey: Gophers and Rock Hydrax. This method of hunting is startling to those who are unacquainted with the beasts, and many an Eriadoran has suddenly found himself in a brief, bewildering combat at the crest of a hill. Several Dunlanders wear scars on their faces attesting that these chance meetings can be dangerous.
description_summary: A black-and-purple eagle of the eastern Eriador hills that hunts silently and low, skimming ridges to ambush small prey like gophers and rock-conies. Toward travellers: no real threat to people, though its sudden low rush over a hilltop can badly startle the unwary.

name: Hummerhorns
description: Surely one of Endor's most vicious insects, the rare but often lethal Hummerhorn is the stuff of legend. Each weighs 6-8 lbs and has a razor sharp, pointed proboscis of chitin (transparent horn). It gores a victim to death, then feasts on the corpse with three sets of mandibles. They resemble gigantic black and grey wasps, and their only failing is the noise they make whenever they approach.
description_summary: One of the most vicious insects alive — a rare, lethal giant wasp the size of a small animal (6-8 lbs), with a transparent horn-like proboscis it uses to gore prey to death before feeding with three sets of jaws. Its one weakness is the loud hum that heralds its approach. Toward travellers: deadly if it attacks, though its hum gives a moment's warning.

name: Black Swans
description: Black Swans are found from Mordor and northward into Wilderland, as far as the southern reaches of Mirkwood. They are also quite common in the river valleys of Nurad and Rhovanion, especially along the River Anduin. They are large for Swans and have been known to mob and overturn boats, usually because the vessels had come too close to the Swans' huge nests.
description_summary: Large black swans of river valleys from Wilderland south to Mordor's edge, common along the Anduin; they will mob and even capsize boats that stray too near their huge nests. Toward travellers: usually peaceable, but aggressively territorial around their nests on the water.

name: Sturgeon
description: The granddaddy of all freshwater fish, the Sturgeon grows up to 18' long in still waters. It has a scaly skin, which is protected by five rows of bony scutes along its sides. Normally very docile, one of these formidable creatures will only attack if its lair is disturbed. Then, it thrashes about angrily, perhaps ramming or upsetting any nearby river craft. Grey and silver, the Sturgeon resides in shallow seas and rivers, feeding on the worms, mollusks, crustaceans, and small fish that live on or near the bottom. They are prized for their meat and their exceedingly tasty eggs.
description_summary: The giant of freshwater fish, growing up to eighteen feet and armoured with rows of bony plates; normally docile, feeding on the bottom, but it will thrash and ram river-craft if its lair is disturbed. Prized for meat and roe. Toward travellers: harmless unless disturbed, when its sheer size can upset a boat.

name: Gelyngyl
description: Gelyngyl (S. "Green Spiders"; sing. "Galungol") are the largest of the so-called "Animal Spiders"—that is, Spiders that are not monsters. Deep green on top and light green on their undersides, they are difficult to discern in a thick forest. Their coloring, coupled with their arboreal nature, makes them very hard (-20) to discern when they are in their natural habitat. Hiding in dense foliage, the rapacious Galungol hangs above animal trails and other areas where its prey might be active, waiting to drop upon unwary victims. Then it injects a powerful (6th lvl) reduction poison into any soft tissue it can find, immobilizing its potential kill before carrying it off to the Spider's web home. Gelyngyl webs are located in clusters set high in the trees. Unlike most of its relatives, Green Spiders are exceptionally social and are prone to attack and feed in groups of ten or more. Acting in unison, they are capable of surrounding and herding their victims into traps. Each Galungol has a pad on each of its four rear legs, and the Spider can rub its legs together to create the sounds with which it communicates.
description_summary: The largest of the true (non-monstrous) "animal" spiders — deep green above and pale below, near-invisible in forest foliage. It hangs above animal trails and drops onto passing prey, injecting a powerful shrinking venom. Toward travellers: a hidden ambush hunter of the woods, dangerous if it drops unseen onto a passer-by.

name: Cliff Hornets
description: Relatives of the common Mud Dauber Wasp, Cliff Hornets are social insects who build their nest in cracks, crevices, and fissures in the sides of cliffs. Although they will not build nests deep underground, their homes can sometimes be found as much as several hundred feet from a cave entrance if a good nesting site can be found there. These insects are not normally dangerous unless one is an intruder who has blundered into the midst of a nest. Then the outraged Hornets will attack en masse with their sharp, barbless stingers, injecting the victim with a venom that will, at minimum, cause pain and swelling. In addition, many people are allergic to Cliff Hornet venom and may die from its effects. Note: Treat Hornet venom as a first level poison. A normal, nonallergic reaction is -25 to all actions for 1-30 hours. There is a 35% chance (01-35) of allergic reaction, a percentage that may be modified by the victim's Constitution. In the case of an allergic reaction, an incapacitating fever and respiratory distress will afflict the victim for 1-4 days, and five or more stings may cause death in 1 to 10 hours.
description_summary: Social wasp-relatives that nest in cracks and fissures of cliffs, sometimes well toward cave-mouths. Not aggressive unless a nest is disturbed, when the swarm attacks en masse with painful, venomous stings. Toward travellers: a hazard only if one blunders into a nest among the rocks.

name: Chamber Birds
description: White, mute, and almost blind, the odd, fungus-eating Chamber Bird is one of the many strange inhabitants of Moria. They seek out the fungus by their remarkable sense of smell (odd indeed for a bird) and note enemies by their inaudibly high cries, in the manner of bats. Their large, gaping beaks have serrated edges and their bluish talons are both sharp and strong. Totally herbivorous (primarily fungivorous), they rarely attack animals unless surprised or starving, though some instinct for meat seems to remain from a predacious ancestor.
description_summary: A strange, near-blind, mute white bird of Moria that finds the fungus it eats by smell and senses danger by inaudible cries like a bat; serrated beak and strong talons, but almost wholly fungus-eating. Toward travellers: harmless unless surprised or starving, when an old predatory instinct may stir.

name: Green Asps
description: The Green Asp is the only venomous serpent indigenous to Calenardhon (Rohan) and the northern White Mountains. It is a sociable creature (for a snake), making its home in as many as fifty of its fellows in empty Rabbit or Ground Squirrel burrows. On sunny days, the Asp community will come out to sun themselves en masse on the slopes near their subterranean home. Green Asps have dull green skin and are usually fat and torpid, with puffy poison glands below their eyes. They are considered unattractive even by those fond of serpents, and a fetid odor reminiscent of rotting strawberries does nothing to relieve this impression. The (5th lvl) bite is rarely fatal, but the area around the wound becomes necrotic, turning awful shades of violet and yellow-green before sloughing off, usually exposing the bone. In some cases (15% chance, modified by Co bonus) the injection may cause the entire limb to turn gangrenous and fall off; otherwise the victim will be incapacitated for 3-6 days with high fever and hallucinations.
description_summary: A dull-green venomous snake of Rohan and the northern White Mountains — unusually sociable, denning by the dozens in old burrows and basking together on sunny slopes; fat, sluggish and foul-smelling. Toward travellers: venomous if trodden on or cornered, but torpid and easily avoided.

name: Blue Terrapins
description: Blue Terrapins (Q. "Luinikrúmori"; sing. "Luinikrúmor") are only found in thickets or very dense, overgrown swamp areas. Their native home is Fangorn Forest. Small, their shells average 18" across, but they have a 12" neck. Thus, a Blue Terrapin can strike like a snake, delivering a 2nd level muscle poison (with a Critical Strike) that can paralyze a small mammal (up to 40 lbs). If it bites, it will paralyze the area or limb it penetrates, partially paralyzing one adjacent area. The poison wears off in 3 hours.
description_summary: A small swamp turtle of Fangorn, its shell about a foot and a half across, with a long neck it can strike out like a snake to deliver a paralysing venom. Toward travellers: harmless unless handled or stepped on, when its quick, numbing bite is a nasty surprise.

name: Pied Swans
description: Handsome aquatic birds, Pied Swans may be the most intelligent of herbivorous avians. They seem able to remember those who have harmed or helped them (at least for periods of several weeks), and there have been reports of pied Swans leading fog-lost travellers to high ground in marshes. Various rural and primitive peoples, such as the Marshmen of the Nindalf, revere these birds as being sacred to the Vala Yavanna. They are creamy white in color, liberally splotched and speckled with black and gray. Unfortunately, their flesh is tasty and their feathers soft, so that those with more greed than reverence hunt them. Pied Swans are most commonly found in the Nindalf and along the shores of the Anduin in the relatively unspoiled areas of Ithilien.
description_summary: Handsome black-and-white swans, perhaps the cleverest of plant-eating birds — they remember kindness and harm, and are said to have led fog-lost travellers to safety in the marshes; revered as sacred by the Marshmen. Toward travellers: gentle and intelligent, a possible helper to the lost, and sacred to some.

name: Cliff Buzzards
description: The Cliff Buzzard is a large, black-feathered bird of the mountains. It builds its nest on inaccessible cliffs. Often several pairs will nest in the same area, behavior more typical of the Corvidae (crowlike birds) than of the Raptors (Hawks and so on). Cliff Buzzards exhibit a great deal of intelligence (for birds) and will cooperate in hunting. They will band together to mob some large creature and drive it over a cliff so that they may all feast together on the shattered body. Any wounded creature, no matter what its size, may be subject to the unwanted attentions of the Cliff Buzzards. They are most common on the southern slopes of the White Mountains.

description_summary: Large black mountain birds that nest in colonies on sheer cliffs and, unusually, hunt cooperatively — banding together to mob and drive large or wounded creatures off a precipice, then feasting below. Toward travellers: a real danger to the injured, or to anyone caught on a cliff-edge in their territory.

name: Ninfiara Wild Aurochs
description: Great wild oxen of the Ninfiara plains — massive, horned and formidable, untamed forebears of cattle. A charging bull is a peril to any hunter, and even wolves think twice before troubling a herd.
description_summary: Great wild oxen of the plains — massive and horned, the untamed ancestors of cattle; a charging bull is a peril to any hunter, and even wolves think twice before troubling a herd. Toward travellers: leaves people alone, but immensely dangerous if a bull charges.

name: Orao
description: The Desert Orao is the principal native Eagle of Far Harad. It is quite similar to the Eagles of the North, except that the environment has forced him to play the role of scavenger as well as hunter. These soaring hunters swoop to attack smaller birds or prey on the ground. Unlike the northern birds, the desert Eagle will as readily eat carrion as fresh meat, an adaptation made necessary by the environment. They are aggressive enough to strike travellers along Harad's southern caravan roads. Aside from Men, the Orao are the dominant hunters on the vast plains of southwestern Harad. Their eyries are set in the caverns of the northern Yellow Mountains, and in wind-hollowed caves the highest of the hilly Raj region to the north. In size, they are no match for the Great Eagles, but they are large enough to carry off a shepherd's goat or lamb.
description_summary: The great desert eagle of Far Harad — like the northern eagles but forced to scavenge as well as hunt, taking prey in the air or on the ground and eating carrion; bold enough to strike at people on the southern caravan roads. Toward travellers: unusually aggressive for an eagle, and may harass or attack those crossing its desert.

name: Nethairin
description: The Plains Snake (Si. "Nathair"; pl. "Nethairin") is roughly six feet long. It has a thick triangular head and a cream-colored body overlaid with a dark diamond pattern. Hiding in shallow burrows and hunting amidst the tall grass of the steppes or plains, the serpent is a quiet creature that relies on stealth and patience to defeat its foes. Rodents, birds, and small lizards make up its diet. Although ostensibly cunning, the Nathair angers easily and will vigorously defend itself rather than flee, even in a fruitless confrontation. Its venom is rather toxic (level 6); symptoms include swelling, weak pulse, shock, and wide discolorations.
description_summary: A six-foot plains snake with a thick triangular head and a dark diamond pattern on cream, hunting rodents and birds by stealth in the tall grass. Quick to anger, it stands and fights rather than flees, and its venom is highly toxic. Toward travellers: dangerous if disturbed in the grass — it defends itself fiercely rather than fleeing.

name: Nethairin Erdyr
description: The Nethairin Erdyr (sing. "Nathair Ardor") live in burrows beneath rocks or within caverns and favor highland locales or the cool wastelands of the North. While very similar in coloration to the Nathair of the Plains, the Nathair Ardor attains a length of only 2-3 feet. It angers easily and will defend itself viciously. Its poison is similar to that of the common Nathair, however it is weaker (5th lvl) and takes longer (6 vs 2 minutes) to affect the victim.
description_summary: A smaller highland cousin of the plains snake (2-3 feet), denning under rocks and in caves of cold uplands and northern wastes; easily angered and quick to bite, with a venom weaker and slower-acting than its lowland kin's. Toward travellers: a venomous hazard among highland rocks, defending itself viciously.

name: Nethraich
description: The Nathrach (pl. "Nethraich") is an aquatic snake that resides in relatively placid or stagnant pools, marshes, and lakes. Brown bands surround its rust-colored body, which typically measures three to five feet in length. Contrary to popular belief, this serpent does not normally strike in water; most victims are on dry land or a marsh island when they stray into a snake run. These snakes only attack when their "runs" are encountered, and then it bites with astounding quickness. A Nathrach's critical strike is accompanied by a muscle poison that causes bleeding, bruises, and swelling (double all these effects on this and subsequent criticals). The venom is not very toxic (level 2) but is designed to stun small rodents. The Arnathrach (pl. "Ernaithrach"), or Royal Nathrach, is a much larger cousin that inhabits the brackish waters near the coasts.
description_summary: A rust-brown banded water-snake of still pools, marshes and lakes, three to five feet long; it rarely strikes in water — victims are usually on land when they cross its "runs," where it bites with startling speed and a bleeding venom. Toward travellers: a hidden danger along marsh-paths rather than in open water.

name: Otrovativi
description: The Otravati (Ap. "Poison"; pl. "Otrovativi") is a small snake found in western Harad. Rarely exceeding two feet in length, it has a murky grey coloring and is easy to overlook. It is, however, not an animal to take lightly. The Otravati's narrow head opens to reveal a surprisingly large mouth full of tiny needle-like teeth. Each tooth is an injector that delivers a dose of the snake's poison on any successful bite (whenever a critical is scored). Locals consider amputation to be the only effective remedy when confronted by the snake's potent (25th lvl) venom, a nerve poison that attacks through the bloodstream and the nervous system simultaneously. The venom is lethal within one hour; only immediate removal of the bitten member will serve as a cure. It is no wonder that the snake bears a name which translates as "poison."
description_summary: A small, easily-overlooked grey snake of western Harad, rarely two feet long, whose wide mouth holds tiny needle-teeth that each inject venom; its poison is so potent that locals reckon amputation the only sure cure for a bite. Toward travellers: deceptively deadly — a drab little snake with one of the most lethal bites.

name: Caru
description: The Caru (Du. "Elk") is a large brown Deer commonly found in northern Eriador, but ranging throughout the cooler parts of northwestern Middle-earth. It is the only Deer species, save the Losrandir, in which both the males and females sport antlers. Caru travel in huge herds of one to twenty thousand and range over vast areas of open country or broken woodlands. Feeding on grass and other tundra plants in the summer, some roam well into the Far North.
description_summary: A large brown deer of northern Eriador and the cool northwest — one of the few deer in which both sexes bear antlers — moving in immense herds, thousands strong, across open country and broken woodland, some ranging far into the North. Toward travellers: harmless; a vast migrating herd is a stirring sight on the open lands.

name: Zamkavi
description: The Zamka (pl. "Zamkavi") is common to the dry hills of western Harad. Its name translates as "snare," and is derived from this snake's method of attack. The Zamka whips its tail out like a lash and coils about a limb or appendage. The snake's long (7') body is lined with small barbs along each side. The barbs are coated with a poisonous secretion which enters through the skin of its victim. This (7th lvl) muscle poison essentially paralyzes the prey (duration = 20 minutes per ten points of RR failure). When its venom takes effect—in forty-five seconds—the serpent will move to constrict its prey. Once the victim is dead, the Zamka remains with the corpse until time and weather has softened it, then begins its feast. Other animals steer well clear of any kill it guards. Note: the Entangle Table is used with any critical indicating a poisoning; it aims at bare skin, will attack while the victim is moving, and continues until movement ceases.
description_summary: A seven-foot snake of the dry western Harad hills, named "snare" for its method — it lashes out a barbed tail to coil round a limb, the barbs delivering a paralysing venom through the skin, then constricts its immobilised prey. Toward travellers: a dangerous ambusher whose whipping, barbed tail can entangle and poison.

name: Mulkánar
description: Largest member of the mussel family, the Mulkana is a giant, hard-shelled bivalve with a black shell that protects its soft, white body. This molusk is related to the Giant Clam, although it is larger, quicker, and has a long, vaguely oval, shell. It secretes a foul, acidic, ink (3rd lvl) which stuns larger prey, kills smaller victims, and slowly eats away skin. Found off the eastern shores of Endor, Mulkanar are hunted for their huge (and reputedly enchanted) pearls. At least one of these precious jewels, the priceless Womaw Moon, exceeds six inches in diameter. Legend proclaims that the larger of these pearls serve as Seeing-stones, although none is as powerful as a Numenorean Palantir.
description_summary: The largest of all mussels — a giant black-shelled bivalve of the eastern shores, prized for its huge, reputedly enchanted pearls; it squirts a foul acidic ink that stuns large prey and dissolves flesh. Toward travellers: no hunter, but its acidic spray and the lure of its great pearls make harvesting it perilous.

name: Nímaeargyrth
description: Elves call the deadliest of Endor's Sharks Nímaeargurth: "White Seadeath" (pl. "Nímaeargyrth"). Typically large (15+ feet) and always hungry, these unsleeping killers hunt virtually anything that has flesh that they can tear apart and eat. Even wounded Whales have felt the wrath of these white (underparts) and grey (overparts) denizens of the sea. The Nímaeargurth's long, pointed snout is equipped with nine rows of flat, triangular teeth. Those of the outermost row are six to eight inches long. When broken off, the teeth are replaced by those of the next row, enabling the Shark to replenish its saw-like weaponry. Like all Sharks, the Nímaeargurth is a cartilage-skeletoned fish. It bears live young, usually in litters of 7-9 Sharklings.
description_summary: The deadliest sharks of the seas — "White Sea-death" to the Elves — large (fifteen feet and more), tireless and always hungry, tearing apart anything with flesh, even wounded whales, with rows of replaceable triangular teeth. Toward travellers: a lethal hunter of any open or coastal water; nothing afloat or swimming is safe near it.

name: Ûlcercain
description: Úlceracin (sing. "Úlcarcan") are large, red, centipede-like creatures that move in hordes across the dense forests of southeastern Endor. Nocturnal creatures, they feed on rotting vegetation, insects, carrion, and (occasionally) live prey. They are extremely quick and are capable of scaling trees and swimming across placid water. Like ants, they operate in rigidly-defined social groups, if necessary swarming to defend their lairs. Úlcercain generally reside in rotting logs. Most of the so-called "Evil Fangs" are harmless to the Children of Ilúvatar; however, the "warriors" that comprise about twenty percent of any colony will attack virtually anything and anyone. Immobilized or sleeping folk need be careful around these creatures. Armed with powerful 2-3 inch mandibles and a mild (lvl 0) nerve venom, a group of Warrior Úlcercain can slay and clean a Man in a matter of minutes.
description_summary: Large red, centipede-like swarm-creatures of southeastern forests — fast, tree-climbing, able to swim, living in rotting logs and moving in ant-like social hordes. Most are harmless to people, but the "warrior" caste that guards the swarm is venomous. Toward travellers: a crawling horde best left alone; its warriors bite with poison if the swarm is disturbed.

name: Goral
description: Goral are a wild, golden bighorn Sheep found exclusively in the Downs of Eriador. They have long, curving, whitish horns. Solitary and intractable, they are unique among Sheep. Goral only gather during mating season, when small groups of males join and then seek out the females. Ranked by the size of their horns, the males instinctively know their place, so there is little fighting during these brief unions. After mating, the females travel as a herd with the young for a couple of months and then scatter. Goral have been very profitably interbred with domestic Sheep, producing excellent varieties of wool-bearing species (Q. "Toa" = "Wool").
description_summary: A wild golden bighorn sheep found only in the Downs of Eriador, with long curving pale horns — solitary and intractable, gathering only to mate, and prized for cross-breeding with domestic flocks. Toward travellers: harmless and aloof, a rare handsome sight on the high downs.

name: Wild Goats
description: Essentially descendants of various escaped domestic varieties, the Wild Goats have dirty, shaggy coats and short, black horns. They range all over Middle-earth. Their small, sharp hooves make them agile climbers, and they can swim remarkably well; however, they restrict the beasts' movements in the boglands, where wide feet provide stability in the muck.
