description_summary: Shaggy, black-horned goats descended from escaped domestic stock, ranging all over Middle-earth — nimble climbers and strong swimmers, though slowed in boggy ground. Toward travellers: harmless; a sign of rough, broken country.

name: Dappled Deer
description: Akin to the Roe Deer (Fiara) of Eriador, the Dappled Deer is small, hardy, and pugnacious. Only the stags fight, but they are exceptionally skiddish and aggressive, especially in Spring. They usually employ their tough horns, which can effectively penetrate mail. Nocturnal creatures, Dappled Deer browse for food at night. They feed on shrubs and broad-leafed trees. During breeding season, males mark their territory by scratching the bark off trees. Note: The Dúnadan Rangers have frequently used this trait to their advantage by disguising their own trail markings as Deer scratches; only the Rangers understand the subtle differences.
description_summary: A small, hardy, pugnacious roe-like deer that browses by night; only the stags fight, skittish and aggressive in spring, using mail-piercing horns and marking territory by scratching bark — a sign the Dúnadan Rangers have learned to imitate to hide their own trail-marks. Toward travellers: shy but feisty; a startled stag can lash out, and its bark-scratches may not be what they seem.

name: Losrandír
description: The Losrandir is a hardy, gregarious Deer with a long, low profile and large antlers. They come in many colors—everything from white to jet black—but most are grey or silver. Unlike those of other Deer, the low forward tine of its antlers is branched. Both males and females bear antlers, although those of the female are usually smaller. Female Losrandír (aka "Lesrandír") move in large herds while males travel in small groups or, in some cases, alone. Wolves and Bears prey on weaker individuals, but the Losrandir's greatest natural enemy is undoubtedly Man. The Lossoth of northwestern Middle-earth and the Urd of the northeast hunt the graceful creature for its fur, meat, and utility as a beast of burden.

description_summary: A hardy, gregarious deer with a long low body and large branched antlers borne by both sexes, ranging from white to black but mostly grey or silver; females move in great herds, males alone or in small groups. Hunted by wolves, bears and above all Men. Toward travellers: harmless; a familiar sight on the cold northern ranges, herded and hunted by the local peoples.

name: White Wolves
description: Northerly kin of the Dire Wolves (S. Drauglos, Q. Lossanarmo), distantly related to the common Grey Wolf and true denizens of the cold, thriving at temperatures as low as -60 degrees F. Most at home in the Far North, they have been seen as far south as the Shire in Eriador and the Celduin Valley in Rhovanion during especially bad winters. The harsh fight to survive makes them even cleverer and bolder than Dire Wolves, preying on everything from rodents and carrion to well-armed Lossoth, with a glistening coat of hollow hairs, two-chambered nostrils and furred feet fit for ice and snow.
description_summary: Northern kin of the dire wolves, built for brutal cold with hollow-haired coats and furred feet — even cleverer and bolder than their southern cousins, preying on anything from rodents and carrion to armed men, and drifting south in harsh winters. Toward travellers: a cunning, fearless pack-hunter of the snows, dangerous to lone or weakened folk.

name: Ants
description: Industrious ants whose great earthen nests dot wood and plain; most are harmless, but the biting and soldier kinds swarm in defense, and the largest southern species can strip a carcass with frightening speed.
description_summary: Industrious ants whose great earthen nests dot wood and plain; most harmless, but the biting and soldier kinds swarm to defend, and the largest southern species can strip a carcass with frightening speed. Toward travellers: a nuisance at most, unless one disturbs an aggressive or southern nest.

name: Water Mammals
description: The otters, minks, beavers and water-rats of river and lake-shore — sleek swimmers that fish, forage and burrow along the banks. Mostly shy of Men, their dams, slides and tracks mark every healthy waterway.
description_summary: The otters, minks, beavers and water-rats of river and lake-shore — sleek swimmers that fish, forage and burrow along the banks. Toward travellers: shy and harmless; their dams, slides and tracks mark a healthy waterway.

name: Crebain
description: Large black crows with evil natures, Crebain (sing. "Craban") have often been servants of evil. They are primarily carrion eaters, and they are not picky about how long their meals have been dead or even about whether they are truly dead yet. They will also mob injured creatures and, if under instructions from some evil intelligence, uninjured individuals travelling alone or lost. Keen-sighted and attracted by shining or brightly-colored objects, they make excellent spies. They are most common on south-central Middle-earth, including Fangorn and Dunland, but may be found elsewhere, especially near centers of evil.
description_summary: Large black crows of evil bent, often servants of dark powers — carrion-eaters that mob the injured, or hunt lone and lost travellers on an evil master's command; keen-eyed and drawn to bright things, they make excellent spies. Toward travellers: an ill sign — their wheeling flocks may be the Enemy's eyes, and they harry the helpless.

name: Land Tortoises
description: Land Tortoises were once common in southern Eriador and western Gondor, particularly in the Gwathló basin. Unfortunately, they are extremely tasty and nutritious, so they are favored by Eriadoran hunters and are nearly extinct. Their beautiful green and grey shells are now exceptionally rare. Prized for their value as armor (the shell makes excellent reinforcement) and (when ground) as a source of medicine to combat blood diseases and ailments, a Land Tortoise shell can bring up to two hundred gold pieces on the open market. Very slow and inoffensive, the Land Tortoise's primary defense is its size: the average specimen is five feet long and three high, weighing about three hundred pounds; a few older ones reach six feet and over a quarter ton. When attacked they retreat until cornered, then ground themselves and pull into their shells, or lash out with their necks and hard, sharp beaks. They feed on nearly every type of vegetation and roam the highlands for richer grass or shrubs. Females dig holes along river banks or on the seashore, lay 11-20 eggs, cover them with moist soil, and abandon them.
description_summary: Slow, inoffensive land tortoises with prized green-and-grey shells, once common in southern Eriador and western Gondor, now near-extinct — hunted for tasty meat, for shells valued as armour, and for ground shell used against blood-ailments. Toward travellers: utterly harmless; valuable chiefly as a rare find.

name: Ulmodili
description: No water creature is as intelligent or blessed as the Ulmodili (Q. "Friend of Ulmo"; pl. "Ulmodili"), the beautiful blue and white Dolphin (Porpoise). Seaborne mammals, they are servants of Ulmo (as opposed to Ossë) and fast allies of the Teleri. Their complex society and wide-ranging schools enable them to act with startling unity when summoned by their master or friends, or when aroused by their enemies. The gregarious Ulmodili live in hierarchical groups of 10-200 individuals, which in turn join to form schools of 100-2000 creatures. Playful and agile, they roll and leap along the surface of the open sea, moving at speeds of up to 40 miles an hour. When they submerge, they can dive as deep as 1000 feet and stay down for as long as five minutes before they need to resurface (they breathe through lungs, not gills). An Ulmodili has smooth blue (top) and white (bottom) skin and a long beaked snout. This snout is a formidable, mace-like weapon that contains an array of short, sharp teeth. Its bulging forehead encases a pad of fat that houses a sonar-like organ that enables the creature to detect objects with stunning accuracy.
description_summary: Beautiful blue-and-white dolphins — the most intelligent and blessed of sea-creatures, servants of the Lord of Waters and friends of the Sea-elves, living in great playful, hierarchical schools that can act with sudden unity. Toward travellers: friendly and benevolent to seafarers, sometimes aiding ships or the drowning.

name: Petlavi
description: The Petla (pl. "Petlavi"), or "Forest-strangler," of southwestern Endor is commonly found in the scattered woodlands of Far Harad (particularly in the Forest of Tears). It haunts the oases between Umbar and Drel, lurking in the branches and pools that the Haradrim prize so dearly. One of Middle-earth's most fearsome reptiles, the Petla is an able hunter who has perfected the arts of camouflage and ambush. It is mottled brown in color and blends perfectly with woodland hues. Dropping from above or entangling its prey in water, the snake attacks by using its powerful jaws to gain a grip and then coils its length around its hapless victim. Petlavi reach lengths exceeding thirty feet (one specimen measured forty-five), are over a foot wide at their thickest, and have been known to swallow a whole goat. Their principal weapon is their coiling, constricting body: after a grip is taken (typically after a "B" critical), the body coils around the target over one to ten rounds; it is extremely hard (-30) to break free, and the victim perishes by suffocation in three to thirty minutes. Defenseless against groups, the snake will not often stand and fight when confronted by more than one enemy.
description_summary: A "forest-strangler" — a fearsome ambush snake of the oases and scattered woods of Far Harad, mottled brown for perfect camouflage. It drops from branches or strikes from pools, gripping prey in powerful jaws and constricting. Toward travellers: a hidden killer of waterside woods, striking from above or below before it is seen.

name: Golodos
description: A Golodo is a bright green, moderately large, flightless bird, rather like a large goose on stilts. Excellent swimmers, they subsist primarily on fish, crustaceans, and shellfish. Golodos gather in large flocks during the breeding season in early summer, traveling in small groups during the rest of the year. Golodos will immediately flee the scent of any predator with surprising, if ungainly speed, except during the nesting season, when they will bravely defend their eggs. Given to snapping up carrion, Golodos enjoy the taste of meat; however, they will eat anything, since they are poor hunters.
description_summary: A bright green, flightless bird like a long-legged goose — an excellent swimmer living on fish and shellfish, fleeing predators with surprising clumsy speed but bravely defending its eggs in nesting season. Toward travellers: harmless and timid, except when guarding a nest.

name: Dumbledoors
description: Great droning bumble-bees of meadow and heath, furred and harmless, blundering from flower to flower. An old country name for them is 'dumbledors', and their deep hum is one of the sounds of a warm summer's day.
description_summary: Great droning bumble-bees of meadow and heath — furred, harmless, blundering from flower to flower; their deep hum is one of the sounds of a warm summer's day. Toward travellers: entirely harmless, a peaceful note of the countryside.

name: Pike
description: The Pike is vicious, and is renowned as the shark of the freshwaters. It has a powerful jaw and wicked teeth, and once it has a hold it will not let go unless it is seriously wounded or it succeeds in biting off a healthy piece of its victim. Pike feed on crustaceans and shellfish when young, graduating to larger prey as they age. Adults eat fish and small mammals. Fortunately, Pike rarely attack a swimmer. This is particularly reassuring to the Rivermen of Calenardhon (Rohan), since Pike grow up to 7' in length in the Onodló (Entwash). Note: Once bitten, a victim will not be attacked again, but they must roll an additional attack to knock the fish loose, with no subtractions for DB, armor etc.
description_summary: A vicious freshwater fish — "the shark of the rivers" — with powerful jaws and wicked teeth that lock on and won't let go, growing up to seven feet in some rivers; it rarely attacks swimmers, preying on fish and small mammals. Toward travellers: little threat to people, but a fierce fighter and a danger to small creatures at the water's edge.

name: Pronghorns
description: Swift, antelope-like grazers of the open steppe and plain, among the fastest beasts of Middle-earth. They run in skittish herds and rely on speed and keen eyes to outpace any pursuer.
description_summary: Swift antelope-like grazers of the open steppe, among the fastest beasts of Middle-earth, running in skittish herds that trust to speed and sharp eyes to outpace any pursuer. Toward travellers: harmless; a fleeting sight bounding away across the plains.

name: Leeches
description: Blood-sucking worms of still and sluggish water, fastening unseen to any warm-blooded thing that wades the shallows. A nuisance of fen and marsh, and in foul waters they gather thick enough to weaken a wading beast.
description_summary: Blood-sucking worms of still, sluggish water that fasten unseen to anything warm-blooded wading the shallows. Toward travellers: a foul nuisance of fen and marsh — in bad waters thick enough to weaken a wading beast.

name: Small Animals
description: The small creatures of field, hedge and burrow — hares, foxes, squirrels, weasels, mice and the like. Seldom a threat, they are the everyday life of the land and the prey on which larger hunters depend.
description_summary: The small creatures of field, hedge and burrow — hares, foxes, squirrels, weasels, mice and the like. Toward travellers: seldom any threat; the everyday life of the land, and the prey larger hunters depend on.

name: Angusaiweli
description: Found virtually anywhere in Middle-earth, save in the coldest climes, the Angusaiwë (Q. "Iron Unbirds"; pl. "Angusaiwéli") is the most resilient, advanced, and common of the larger Rávatsar. This is due to the fact that they are, unlike any other reptile or amphibian, warm-blooded. The Angusaiwéli constitute a unique branch of the Rávatsar and are closely related to flightless birds; thus their name. There are nine species: those that run on four legs (three species), those that run on two legs (five species), and those that hop (one species). All are deep grey or black with bright red eyes. The two-legged species walk upright using their tails for balance and can travel over rugged terrain at speeds exceeding 35 miles an hour. Poisonous carnivores, Unbirds hunt lizards, frogs, birds, large insects, and small mammals, swallowing smaller prey whole. Against larger foes they bare hollow, needle-like fangs and inject a potent (3rd lvl) venom that immobilizes (1-100 rds with RR failure 01-65) or kills (1-10 rds with RR failure 66+). Aside from their speed, they rely on cunning and horny body armor, with a ridge of pointed scales and scales as tough as iron.
description_summary: "Iron Unbirds" — unique warm-blooded reptiles akin to flightless birds, found almost everywhere but the coldest lands; deep grey or black with bright red eyes, in kinds that run on four legs, run on two, or hop. Toward travellers: a strange, hardy beast of the wilds; most are no great threat, though their oddness unsettles.

name: Snowhare
description: The snow hare is one of the largest members of the rabbit family. It inhabits the upper elevations of the Misty Mountains where there is snow cover year round. The animal's silvery white pelt, prized for its warmth, provides excellent camouflage for the furry mountain denizen. A larger, more fearsome variety of the snow hare has been rumored to exist lurking close to the opening of high mountain caves. These creatures seem to exhibit a cruel intelligence and are rumored to frenzy upon provocation. This description was given by a traveller after returning half-crazed from the mountains: "It had huge ... well I mean gigantic ... just big teeth."
description_summary: One of the largest of the rabbit kind, dwelling in the year-round snows of the high Misty Mountains, its silvery-white pelt prized for warmth and perfect for camouflage. A larger, fiercer kind is rumoured near high cave-mouths, said to show cruel cunning and to frenzy when provoked. Toward travellers: the common kind is harmless game; the rumoured greater sort is a half-legendary terror of the heights.

name: Sarnúmeni
description: Distantly related to the Carnantor, the Sarnúmeni (Q. "West-stones"; sing. "Sarnúmen") are large, hard-skinned, freshwater Eels. Their name is derived from their grey, horny scales, which lend a Sarnúmen tremendous protection and enable them to burrow in riverbeds, camouflaging themselves as rocks. Since the mid-Second Age, when they came from their original home in Númenor and were carelessly introduced into the rivers and lakes of southern Endor, they have become the dominant hunter in the watershed of the Yellow Mountains (S. "Ered Laranor"; Q. "Orolanari"). Aside from great strength and huge teeth, Sarnúmeni are noted for having deadly barbs along their spine. These barbs are connected with poison glands, and anyone stepping on or handling one of these formidable Eels is in danger of being injected with a bluish nerve poison named for the Sarnúmen. Herbalists have been known to refine this liquid, creating a lethal (30th) poison that is feared all over southern Middle-earth. Fortunately, the liquid has an exceedingly limited life of its own and is extremely hard (-30) to handle without exposing oneself to its effects.
description_summary: Large, armour-skinned freshwater eels whose grey horny scales let them burrow in riverbeds and lie disguised as stones; brought long ago from Númenor and now the dominant hunters of the southern Yellow Mountain rivers. Toward travellers: a hidden ambusher of the riverbed — what looks like a rock may strike.

name: Fintónarki
description: The Fintónarki, or Wild Goats, are odd beasts found on the plains south of Mirkwood. They have several characteristics that set them apart from the other Goats of Middle-earth. For one thing, they are sometimes found in mixed herds with the Wild Horses of the area, an arrangement found nowhere else. Second, they are almost untamable, although it is reported that Elves may have some success at this task. Third, they are immune to all naturally occuring ingestible poisons and hence can live on almost anything that contains nourishment. Finally, these strange creatures have an unquenchable desire to be scratched on the head, between their long, dangerous horns. It is rumored among various denizens of the Talath Harroch region (particularly those with small, naïve children) that if one should do one of these hoofed hedonists the favor of scratching it in the proper spot, it will become ever loyal to the person who scratches it. This tale has caused numerous injuries to youngsters seeking an unusual pet.
description_summary: Odd wild goats of the plains south of Mirkwood — near-untamable, sometimes grazing in mixed herds with wild horses, and immune to all natural ingested poisons so they can feed on almost anything. Toward travellers: harmless and intractable, a curiosity of the eastern plains.

name: Corpse Candles
description: Corpse Lanterns, being both stronger and more i n t e l l i g e n t than Corpse Candles, weave a stronger and more alluring web, appearing whole and wholesome above the waters of the marsh or swamp, and seeming to the victim to be something that would attract his attention. The visions vary according to the victim, and several unfortunates gazing upon the same Corpse Lantern may see different things: the lusty warrior may see a buxom wench with a lantern, searching for something; the compassionate Healer a lost child with a lamp; the suspicious thief a fat merchant with a purse that could use lightening, engaged in burying more treasure in the safe embrace of the marsh. Thus the Corpse Lantern entices its victims to a death by drowning, enabling the undead remnant to drain 5 Co points per round in the interim.
description_summary: Luring marsh-spirits that appear as a wholesome, alluring vision above the water — each victim sees whatever would draw them in (a fair maid, a lost child, a fat purse) — to lead them to drown in the bog. Toward travellers: a deadly deception of swamp and marsh by night; the beckoning light is a trap.

name: Ghosts
description: These can be the most powerful of undead, save the Nazgûl (see below). They are totally energy, having no physical component at all, and are activated solely by the presence of living beings of the same type that they once were (most often Men). They appear as they remember themselves being, with varying degrees of effectiveness: lesser Ghosts often neglect pertinent details, such as appropriate clothing for the occasion. They also do not realize that, since their appearence is nothing but illusion, their will can affect it. A Ghost who wishes to be larger and more menacing is so, with nothing but a thought. Greater Ghosts, whose instincts are under more control, avoid this giveaway and behave in a very lifelike manner, enabling them to drain the constitution (and hence l i t e energy) of a victim while engaging him in conversation or merely being near him. It is a strange fact that most Ghosts seem not to realize that they are dead; often, if they can be convinced of their true nature, they will dissipate and go forth to their appointed rest. (Note that Elves do not have Ghosts, since their spirits always know where to go. It is notable that Elves do not fear Ghosts, perhaps for this reason). However, Ghosts are remarkably impervious to reason on this score (How would you. Reader, react if someone insisted that you were dead?), and would-be persuaders are well-warned that there is no way that the Ghost can avoid draining their life-energy, any more than they themselves can avoid having their hearts beat. Anyone near enough to a Ghost to speak to it is near enough to be drained. Some Ghosts are bound to a place or object that was very important to them in life or that figured strongly in their deaths: a sentry killed while guarding a crucial post, for instance, whose spirit remains "on duty" long after the fortress that he guarded has fallen to ruins; the murder victim haunting the gold that she was killed for; the child plague victim still holding fast to the wooden doll that comforted its last hours.
description_summary: Among the most powerful undead — beings of pure spirit with no body, roused by the presence of the living of their former kind. They appear as they remember themselves, and can shape that illusion into something larger and more terrible at will. Toward travellers: a hateful, fear-wielding haunt of ruins and old places, perilous to face after dark.

name: Huorn
description: Huorns — trees grown strange and half-awake, between tree and Ent, that can move slowly and feel anger. They gather in the deep shadows of Fangorn and the old forests, and folk who stray among them when they are roused seldom return.
description_summary: Trees grown strange and half-awake — something between tree and Ent — that can move slowly and feel anger, gathering in the deep shadow of Fangorn and the old forests. Toward travellers: those who stray among them when they are roused seldom return.

name: Undead
description: The restless dead in their many forms — wights, ghosts, ghouls, skeletons and worse, animated by old curses, evil will or the malice of the Enemy. Cold, tireless and hateful of the living, they haunt barrows, ruins and battlefields, chiefly by night.

Toward travellers: implacably hostile, they do not bargain, only harm.

name: Giants
description: Massive humanoid creatures found in mountains and wilderness.

description_summary: Massive humanoid beings of the mountains and wild places, far larger and stronger than Men. Toward travellers: dangerous through sheer size and strength — some merely aloof and brutish, others openly hostile.

name: Stone-trolls
description: Trolls that turn to stone when exposed to sunlight, found in rocky areas and hills.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "En Egladil", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Trollshaws", "probability": 4}, {"region": "West Rhudaur", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Eregion", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Forovirian", "probability": 4}, {"region": "Mänoriand", "probability": 6}, {"region": "Central Anduin", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Central Misty Mountains", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Coldfells", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "En Udanoriath", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Ettenmoors", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Mordor", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "North Misty Mountains", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Nurn", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Rivendell", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Wilderness of Arnor", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Dunland", "probability": "4"}, {"region": "Parth Celebrant", "probability": 3}]
description_summary: Huge, brutish trolls that turn to stone in sunlight, lurking in rocky hills and caves and coming out by night; strong and slow-witted but immensely dangerous. Toward travellers: deadly after dark, killing the unwary — but they can be outlasted until dawn turns them to stone.

name: Fell Beasts
description: Small, Dragon-related monsters, Fell Beasts are horrible and terrifying creatures bred from Winged-drakes. Sauron found them roosting in the remote highlands of the North and refined them in order to produce swift, aerial steeds for his Nazgûl.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Central Anduin", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Central Misty Mountains", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Dor Rhunen", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Dorwinion", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Eothraim", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Gramuz", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Nan Anduin", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "North Misty Mountains", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Nurn", "probability": 5}, {"region": "Ronen-in-Anduin", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Mordor", "probability": "4"}, {"region": "Dor Guldur", "probability": "4"}, {"region": "Iron Hills", "probability": 6}]
description_summary: Small dragon-kin — horrible winged monsters bred up from winged-drakes by Sauron to serve as flying steeds for the Nazgûl. Toward travellers: a terror of the sky; their shadow and shriek alone bring dread, and they hunt at their riders' command.

name: Beaded Lizards
description: Stout, venomous lizards of the dry southern scrub, marked with bead-like scales. Slow but ill-tempered, they deliver a painful, lingering bite when handled or trodden on.
description_summary: Stout, venomous lizards of the dry southern scrub, marked with bead-like scales — slow but ill-tempered, delivering a painful, lingering bite when handled or trodden on. Toward travellers: easily avoided, but a nasty venomous bite if disturbed.

name: Cave-trolls
description: Trolls that dwell in caves and underground passages, adapted to darkness.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Evendim Hills", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Fornost", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Forochel", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Lone-lands", "probability": 3}, {"region": "Numeriador", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Rammas Formen", "probability": 3}, {"region": "Talath Muil", "probability": "4"}, {"region": "Weather Hills", "probability": 3}, {"region": "Forovirian", "probability": "4"}, {"region": "Mänoriand", "probability": 6}, {"region": "Central Anduin", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Central Misty Mountains", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Coldfells", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Dunland", "probability": "4"}, {"region": "En Udanoriath", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Ettenmoors", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Moria", "probability": 3}, {"region": "North Misty Mountains", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Parth Celebrant", "probability": "4"}, {"region": "Rivendell", "probability": "3"}, {"region": "Wilderness of Arnor", "probability": "3"}]
description_summary: Trolls adapted to life underground, dwelling in caves and deep passages and at home in darkness — huge, thick-skinned and savage. Toward travellers: a lethal danger in the dark of caverns and old mines, strong enough to crush an armed party.

name: Lassaraukar
description: Leaf-demons (Q. "Lassarauko"), nocturnal wood-spirits seduced and corrupted by Morgoth into savage arboreal predators. Immortal and unbreedable, with hearing and smell beyond Elven sight, they race along branches faster than a Swift flies and can stop instantly only at predetermined spots. Tall, featureless and greenish-hided, they live in "lats" of six (five Lesser led by one Greater) and rest motionless most of the day, often mistaken for statues (their eyes worth 500 gp).
description_summary: "Leaf-demons" — nocturnal wood-spirits twisted by Morgoth into savage tree-top predators; tall, featureless and green-hided, with senses keener than Elven sight, racing along branches faster than a bird and freezing motionless by day, often mistaken for statues. Toward travellers: an almost unkillable forest horror that strikes from the canopy in the dark.

name: Hurndaen
description: Pukel-creatures that look like iron pillars until disturbed, when they become deadly iron manlike forms; like other Pukel they can reconstitute themselves, though this takes at least 50 years of undisturbed repose.
description_summary: Animated guardian constructs that stand like plain iron pillars until disturbed, then move as deadly iron man-shapes; if destroyed they slowly reconstitute over decades of undisturbed rest. Toward travellers: a hidden sentinel — what seems a mere pillar may wake and kill.

name: Mountain Giants
description: Extremely long-lived, reclusive beings of immense size who dwell in the high peaks and avoid contact with other races.
description_summary: Immensely long-lived, reclusive giants of the high peaks who shun other peoples and keep to the heights. Toward travellers: usually avoid all contact, but catastrophic if angered or disturbed in their mountains.

name: Swamp Stars
description: Ghosts that have wholly degenerated into small bright lights which lure the unwary (5th-level Luring) into bogs, quicksand or deep marsh pools, draining life-energy as the victim drowns or is immobilised.
description_summary: Ghosts wholly decayed into small bright lights that lure the unwary into bogs, quicksand and deep pools, draining their life as they drown or sink. Toward travellers: a beckoning light over the marsh at night that means only death.

name: Tom Bombadil
description: The oldest being, who sings songs to calm the angry trees of the forest.
description_summary: The oldest of beings — a merry, mysterious master of his own small country who sings to quiet the angry trees and fears nothing in the world, wholly his own master and untouched by the powers that move others. Toward travellers: kindly and helpful to those who stumble upon him, offering shelter, song and rescue — but bound to no one's errand but his own.

name: Half-trolls
description: Half-trolls are the product of a union of Olog-hai and Variag Men. For this reason, they are sometimes confused with Black Trolls, but they are smaller and quicker and vaguely resemble Men. Their 7' height, jet-black skin, long sharp red tongues, and glowing red eyes should also serve notice of their uniqueness. Half-trolls differ from Olog-hai in other ways: (1) they wear crude black clothing and considerable amounts of armor; (2) they are kept away from other Trolls; (3) they are as intelligent as Men; and (4) they are more agile. Given their increased minds, they get two background points.
description_summary: A man-like troll-breed — about seven feet, jet-black skinned with red eyes and long red tongues, smaller and quicker than full trolls and as clever as Men, going armoured in crude black gear. Toward travellers: dangerous, disciplined fighters, cunning enough to ambush and fight like soldiers rather than mere brutes.

name: Olog-hai (Black Trolls)
description: The Olog-hai have been bred by Sauron from lesser Troll stock, and have until late been a rare breed. Cunning and organized - yet as big and strong as their lesser brethren - the Olog-hai are superb Warriors. They know no fear, and thirst for blood and victory. Olog-hai are also called Black Trolls, for they have black scaly hides and black blood. Most carry blank shields and war hammers, although they are adept at using almost any weapon. They differ from older Troll varieties in other ways as well: (1) they ignore bleeding or stun results when given a critical strike from a normal weapon, since they are more resistant to unenchanted weapons; (2) they can operate freely in daylight; (3) they are relatively quick; and (4) they are capable of using normal language properly, and speak Black Speech (Rank 5) and Westron (Rank 3).
description_summary: Sauron's elite troll-breed — as huge and strong as common trolls but cunning, organized and fearless, with black scaly hides and black blood, bearing war-hammers and shields; unlike older trolls they endure sunlight and shrug off wounds. Toward travellers: superb, bloodthirsty warriors that know no fear, among the deadliest foes on any road near the Enemy's lands.

name: Black Demons
description: Lesser Maiar allied with Morgoth whose forms and abilities vary infinitely. They can sometimes be persuaded to aid Men or Elves, even in good deeds, but unless the petitioner is carefully protected and binds them with strong constraints little good comes of such bargains; like Balrogs, many partake of natural forces such as fire.
description_summary: Lesser Maiar of Morgoth's allegiance whose shapes and powers vary without limit, many bound up with raw forces like fire, as Balrogs are. Toward travellers: terribly dangerous; they may sometimes be bargained with, but such bargains rarely end well for the one who makes them.

name: Balrogs
description: Evil Spirits of Fire (S. "Demons of Might"), among Morgoth's first allies in the early days of Arda; immortal and singular. The Balrog of Moria (Durin's Bane), perhaps the last of his kind, was roused by deep-delving Dwarves, slew two Dwarf-kings and drove the rest from Moria, and was finally vanquished by Gandalf. Its First Age kin Gothmog and the White Balrog Lungorthin are long gone.
description_summary: Ancient demons of fire and shadow — among Morgoth's first and mightiest servants, immortal and singular. Towering, wreathed in flame and darkness, wielding fiery whip and sword; the Balrog of Moria slew Dwarf-kings and emptied the city before Gandalf destroyed it. Toward travellers: catastrophic and all but unkillable — a force to flee, not fight.

name: Cave Drakes
description: Cave-drakes (Q. "Rondoleki") are relatively small, ancient wingless Dragons that rely on instinct rather than plots, hunting near their high-mountain cavern lairs with short powerful legs and muscular bodies.
description_summary: Small, ancient wingless dragons that hunt on instinct near their high mountain-cavern lairs, with short powerful legs and muscular bodies. Toward travellers: a deadly ambush predator of the deep hills — less cunning than the great dragons, but lethal.

name: Skeletons
description: Skeletons are similar to Ghouls, except that they have been picked clean of all flesh. Somehow their bleached bones hang together, animated by evil forces. While not as inherently disgusting as Ghouls, the sight of an animated Skeleton bearing weapons is still terrifying. The common Skeleton attacks alter the manner of a Ghoul, rushing headlong at its foe and using i t s teeth and claws. Skeleton Lords often bear the weapons that were buried w i t h them and can utilize simple magical items. Having no organs or blood. Skeletons are not affected by bleeding criticals, and all Puncture criticals become Crushes.
description_summary: Animated bare bones, flesh long gone, held together by evil power — they rush at the living with teeth and claws, and their lords still bear grave-weapons and simple magic; they feel no pain or bleeding. Toward travellers: a terrifying, relentless attacker, unsettling but mindless.

name: Nazgûl
description: The Nazgul or Ringwraiths, true Lords of the Undead and the single most powerful beings of their kind ever to walk Middle-earth; the nine Wraith-kings serve from Mordor (Minas Morgul) and Dol Guldur. (Detailed in ICE's Peoples of Middle-earth series.)
description_summary: The Ringwraiths — nine wraith-kings, the mightiest undead ever to walk Middle-earth, serving the Dark Lord from Minas Morgul and Dol Guldur; cloaked and unseen, they wield paralysing terror. Toward travellers: an overwhelming, soul-freezing dread — to be hunted by one is near-certain doom.

name: Marsh-Drakes
description: Marsh-drakes are small Fire-drakes of the wetlands whose extremely toxic secretions act like diluted Dragon-blood.
description_summary: Small fire-drakes of the wetlands whose secretions are extremely toxic, like diluted dragon-blood. Toward travellers: a deadly lurker of fen and bog whose very fluids burn and poison.

name: Colbran
description: Pukel-creatures, the strongest and most clever works of the Druedain and the Daen Coentis: animated stone or metal that stays unseen until activated, then takes the form of attenuated manlike beings glowing with fierce blue light, some casting lightning bolts.
description_summary: The greatest constructs of the Wild-men — animated stone or metal that lies unseen until roused, then rises as a slender man-shape glowing with fierce blue light, some hurling lightning. Toward travellers: a hidden guardian that wakes to strike; what seemed dead stone comes alive in blue fire.

name: Land Drakes
description: Land-drakes are one of the three wingless varieties of Cold-drake (Earth element), keeping to northern highlands and plains.
description_summary: Wingless cold-drakes of the earth, keeping to the northern highlands and plains. Toward travellers: a huge, armoured ground-dragon, deadly if met in its cold country.

name: Barrow Wights
description: Malevolent spirits that haunt the Barrow-downs (Tyrn Gorthad), inhabiting the burial mounds of ancient Men and dressing their victims in grave-treasure.

description_summary: Malevolent grave-spirits that haunt the burial mounds of ancient Men, lying in wait to seize victims and clothe them in grave-treasure for the dead. Toward travellers: a deadly haunt of the barrow-downs that traps and entombs the living, above all after dark.

name: Rain-Drakes
description: Rain-drakes (Q. "Aelinileki") are relatives of the true Water-drakes that dwell in fresh water, spending much time in shallows and building lairs near it.
description_summary: Freshwater kin of the water-drakes, dwelling in shallows and lakes and lairing near water. Toward travellers: a dangerous water-dragon of rivers and meres.

name: Water-Drakes
description: True Water-drakes, often called Sea Serpents, are agile swimming Dragons (freshwater and saltwater) that seize prey with six fin-claws; their one weakness, besides fire, is being lured from water.
description_summary: True water-dragons — often called sea-serpents — agile swimmers of fresh and salt water that seize prey with six fin-claws; their weakness, besides fire, is being lured from the water. Toward travellers: a lethal hunter of lakes, rivers and coasts, safest fought by drawing it onto land.

name: Specters
description: Uncommon in Middle-earth, Specters are cold, white, incorporeal figures akin to Ghosts. Victims killed by Specters frequently become Specters themselves. The Balrog of Moria, for instance, had his minions throw captives into caverns and pits known to be haunted by these creatures. The Specters' need for energy is so great that even the lesser kind drain 8 points of constitution per round. They cannot be affected by nonmagical weapons because of their insubstantial nature. The Specters of Moria can cast Shock Bolts, in addition to their energydraining abilities.
description_summary: Cold, white, incorporeal figures akin to ghosts and hungry for life-energy — their victims often rise as specters in turn; ordinary weapons cannot touch them. Toward travellers: a draining, fear-wielding haunt of pits and haunted depths that mere steel cannot harm.

name: Winged Cold-Drakes
description: Winged Cold-drakes, like Fire-drakes, descend from the true Cold-drakes; they keep to northern mountain lairs (notably the Grey Mountains) and raid Dwarven settlements.
description_summary: Flying cold-drakes of the northern mountains, notably the Grey Mountains, that raid Dwarven halls. Toward travellers: a winged, fireless dragon — vast, cold and rapacious, striking from the peaks.

name: Mewlips
description: hnwmskt-FM,CDHJP,EQRY-8; 6-11 ft Mewlips are an evil, semi-legendary race of exceedingly rare, cannibalistic spirits. These shadowy spirits exist to drink blood, which they need almost as much as other undead need life-energy. Denizens of graveyards, ruins, and other pleasant sites, they are often found surprisingly near inhabited areas, especially if they have not found much sustenance farther afield lately. Mewlips are rare even for Undead. Fond of the most noisome and darkest swamps and marshes, they have a silent tread and strangling hands, although they typically use jagged weapons of rusty steel or stone. They are deceptively human in appearance: however, their backs are horribly hunched and their skin shines with a clammy, greenish-brown palor. Even their ragged clothing is moist and foul. Mewlips feed on most anything and they covet shiny objects, especially things of gold. It is said that such items remind them of uncursed life, of the nature of beingsnot in the thrall of Darkness.
description_summary: A rare, cannibalistic undead spirit that haunts dark swamps, graveyards and ruins by night. Looks almost human but hunched and wrong, with clammy greenish-brown skin and damp, filthy rags; it moves in silence and kills with strangling hands or jagged rusty blades, craving blood and coveting gold. Toward travellers: a lurking predator that stalks and ambushes the isolated, and never parleys.

name: Ghouls
description: Ghouls are disgusting animated corpses that have the lengthened teeth and nails considered typical of a one- or two-month old corpse. They are preserved by the conditions around them, either tannin-loaded swampy water or drying winds, and they are animated by some evil power. Stupid and remembering little if any of their past, Ghouls are dominated by thoughts of hunger for energy and defense of their territory. The average Ghoul simply rushes at its victims, tearing with teeth and nails. Their gruesome appearance i n s t i l l s Fear (victims must make a successful RR or flee), while their filthy and rotting bodies create wounds that often (01-30) become infected. the Undead. They are the single most powerful beings of this nature ever to walk Middle-earth. All nine of these Wraithkings are described in detail in ICE's Lords of Middle-earth, Volume II.
description_summary: Disgusting animated corpses with lengthened teeth and nails, preserved by swamp-water or dry air and driven by hunger and territory; stupid and memoryless, they rush and tear with teeth and claws, and their look instils fear. Toward travellers: a mindless, terrifying attacker of graveyards and waste places after dark.

name: Werewolves
description: Huge, intelligent enchanted wolves first made by Morgoth, able to stand and use clawlike hands, speak, use tools and cast spells but never become Men. Caran-Carach (Red Fang) guarded Dol Guldur, home of the Necromancer, and hunted the Wood-elves and Wood-men of southern Mirkwood.
description_summary: Huge, intelligent enchanted wolves first made by Morgoth — able to stand and use clawed hands, to speak, use tools and cast spells, though never to become Men. Toward travellers: a cunning, sorcerous predator far worse than any natural wolf, hunting the dark woods.

name: Lesinavi
description: Ghoul-like undead that prowl the southern wastes, lairing in the caves of dry hills or among the dunes over unmarked graves; their dried eyes perceive the life-energy of victims as painful light, and they ignore stun and pain.
description_summary: Ghoul-like undead of the southern wastes, lairing in dry-hill caves and dune-graves; their withered eyes see a victim's life-energy as painful light, and they ignore pain and stun. Toward travellers: a relentless desert haunt that hunts by the glow of the living.

name: Cold Drake
description: Cold-drakes (Q. "Helkaloki") are the most common and oldest breed of Dragon, from which all others descend; wingless or winged, they haunt northern mountain caverns and are the Grey Dragons (Sintanguleki) of the Ered Mithrin.
description_summary: The oldest and commonest dragon-breed, ancestor of all others — winged or wingless, fireless but vast and cunning, haunting northern mountain caverns; the Grey Dragons of the Ered Mithrin are of this kind. Toward travellers: an ancient armoured worm, deadly even without fire — a peril to a whole realm.

name: Dragons
description: Dragons and drakes of Middle-earth — the great worms bred by Morgoth in the Elder Days and their lingering descendants. Vast, cunning and malevolent, armoured in scale and (the greatest) gifted with fire and a spellbinding voice; any one is a peril to a whole realm.
description_summary: The great worms bred by Morgoth in the Elder Days and their lingering descendants — vast, cunning and malevolent, scaled in armour and, the greatest of them, gifted with fire and a spellbinding voice. Toward travellers: any one is a peril to a whole kingdom; to meet one is mortal danger.

name: Forest-trolls
description: Trolls that haunt the deep woods — Mirkwood's southern eaves, Fangorn's marches and the tangled forests of the wild lands. Mossy and bark-hided, they blend with the timber until they move, ambushing travellers who stray from the path and dragging them back to their lairs.

description_summary: Trolls of the deep woods — mossy, bark-hided and blending with the timber until they move, lurking on the southern eaves of Mirkwood and the forest marches. Toward travellers: they ambush those who stray from the path and drag them back to their lairs.

name: Fire Drake
description: Fire-drakes (Q. "Uruloki") are the slender, fire-breathing winged Dragons descended from true Cold-drakes; the greatest Worms of the North (including Smaug) belong to this family and lair in the Grey Mountains and Lonely Mountain.
description_summary: The slender, fire-breathing winged dragons descended from cold-drakes — the greatest worms of the North, Smaug among them, lairing in the Grey Mountains and the Lonely Mountain. Toward travellers: the most terrible of dragons; fire, cunning and a deadly voice make any one a doom.

name: Hill-trolls
description: Big, brutish Trolls of the highlands and broken hill-country, met among the crags of the Trollshaws, the Coldfells, the Ettenmoors and the Misty Mountains. Stronger and hardier than Men but dull-witted, they lair in caves and ruins and raid lonely roads by night — and the older breeds must beware the sun that turns them to stone.

Toward travellers: a deadly night-ambush on hill-roads, best escaped until dawn.

name: Ice Drake Gondring
description: Gondring, a great cold-drake of the northern mountains — a wingless dragon whose breath is killing frost rather than fire. It lairs among high snows and frozen caves, and its passing leaves men and beasts rimed and dead.
description_summary: A great wingless cold-drake of the northern mountains whose breath is killing frost rather than fire; it lairs among high snows and frozen caves, leaving men and beasts rimed and dead in its wake. Toward travellers: a named horror of the far peaks whose very breath freezes the life from the living.

name: Lesser Dragon
description: A lesser drake — smaller and less mighty than the great dragons, often wingless or fireless, yet still huge, armoured and deadly. Such worms lair in mountain, marsh and waste, raiding herds and folk and hoarding what plunder they can.
description_summary: A lesser drake — smaller and weaker than the great dragons, often wingless or fireless, yet still huge, armoured and deadly, lairing in mountain, marsh and waste and raiding herds and folk. Toward travellers: still a mortal danger, hoarding what plunder it can take.

name: Ice drake
description: Ice-drakes are Cold-drakes of the Earth element distinguished by an icy breath, dwelling in the frozen reaches of the far North.
description_summary: Cold-drakes of the far North marked by an icy breath, dwelling in the frozen reaches. Toward travellers: a frost-breathing dragon of the polar wastes, deadly in its cold domain.

name: Young Trolls
description: Half-grown Trolls, not yet at full size or strength but already dangerous — clumsier and more reckless than their elders, and often driven from a lair to fend for themselves. Easier prey than a full-grown Troll, but a pack of them is no small peril.

description_summary: Half-grown trolls, not yet at full size but already dangerous — clumsier and more reckless than their elders, often driven from a lair to fend for themselves. Toward travellers: easier prey than a full troll, but a pack of them is no small peril.

name: Trolls
description: Bred by Morgoth in mockery of Ents, Trolls are as tough and stupid as the stone from which they were made. Some feel they are related to Giants. In any case, Trolls hate all other creatures, a legacy of Morgoth's dark touch. Normal Trolls are divided into several types (based on their location): Hill, Cave, Snow, and Stone-trolls. The latter is the most common group. These types all revert to the stone of their substance when exposed to the light of day, for they were created in Darkness and the Sun can unmake them. Physical Character: They are huge and immensely strong, with thick bodies and limbs. Their tough hides have an inconsistent quality; many have overlapping scales and some have body hair. Various shades of brown, green, or grey hide, with black or brown eyes. They have black blood. Average endurance. 9 to 11 feet. Variable lifespan; hundreds of years. Trolls do not understand fear. Their vision is the same as that of Orcs. Sunlight. When exposed to the natural light of day they turn to stone. Trolls live in order to play and eat. To them play means killing and pillaging, and eating means fresh meat - raw or cooked - or jelly made from innards.
