description_summary: A wary snow-people who roam the northern wastes from spring to autumn, camping rough on rocky slopes for days at a time, reserved with strangers and apter to move on than to deal with rough folk. Toward travellers: shy and cautious, but approached gently they can give detailed knowledge of the land, its hazards and its ways.

name: Tinkers and travellers
description: Tinkers and peddlers move from steading to steading along hardpacked and paved roads, doing odd jobs, selling goods and carrying news and gossip. They are described as friendly and talkative, equal in information value to merchants and often more approachable, shaping the social fabric of the hills.[file:179]
description_summary: Tinkers and peddlers who move steading to steading along the roads, doing odd jobs, selling small goods and carrying news and gossip. Toward travellers: friendly and talkative, a ready source of local news and rumour, and welcome company on the road.

name: Rivermen traders
description: River-folk who ply the Baranduin and other northern rivers, claiming the waters as their own and serving trade and communication between Arthedain, Lindon and other regions.[file:25]
description_summary: River-folk who ply the Baranduin and other northern rivers, claiming the waters as their own and carrying trade and word between the northern realms. Toward travellers: independent boat-folk, useful for passage and news, but proprietary about their rivers.

name: Beffraen
description: A relatively primitive folk akin to Drúedain, inhabiting the dark woods of the Rast Vorn peninsula; they are secretive, use the treacherous forest and coasts to remain largely outside Dúnadan control and maintain their own customs.[file:31]
description_summary: A primitive woodland folk akin to the Wild-men, dwelling in the dark forests of a remote peninsula — secretive, using treacherous woods and coasts to stay beyond Dúnadan rule and keep their own ways. Toward travellers: shy and elusive, shunning outsiders; wary in their woods but not aggressors unless threatened.

name: Celebrimbor
description: Master Smith, founder of the Gwaith-i-Mírdain and de facto leader of the craft community; arguably the second-greatest Elven smith after Fëanor, and the maker of the Three Rings.
description_summary: The master-smith who founded the great Elven craft-guild of Eregion — among the greatest smiths of his people and the maker of the Three Elven Rings. Toward travellers: a noble, brilliant craftsman-lord, gracious to the worthy and wholly absorbed in his art.

name: Rohirrim
description: The Rohirrim (S. "Masters of Horses") are the Northmen of Rohan and have a complex ancestry. They have considerable amounts of facial hair. Blond, with fair skin and blue eyes. Herders and horse-masters, they spend much of their year living in various semi-permanent camps set out on a circuit of pastures. They return to their permanent homes for the winter. Some garden or farm. Most Rohirrim are also accomplished hunters and fishermen. The Rohirrim hate the Dunlendings and consider the Woses as lesser beings. They also have an age-old hatred of Orcs, Wargs, Easterlings, and Dragons.
description_summary: The horse-lords of Rohan — tall, fair, blond and bearded Northmen who live much of the year in seasonal pasture-camps, herding, hunting and fishing; proud riders with old hatreds for Dunlendings and orcs. Toward travellers: hospitable to honest, respectful folk, but suspicious of strangers and swift to challenge intruders on their lands.

name: Saralainn
description: Rowdy, semi‑independent clans of Saralainn and the Gwathló mouthlands whose culture blends Dunnish traditions and refugee influences, often providing both raiders and hardy defenders in the south.[file:31]
description_summary: Rowdy, semi-independent clans of the Gwathló mouthlands, their culture a blend of Dunnish ways and refugee influences — providing both raiders and stubborn defenders. Toward travellers: unruly and unpredictable; some hospitable, some quick to rob, depending on the clan and the day.

name: Dunlendings Bards
description: Singers and lore-keepers of the Dunlending clans, carrying the genealogies, feuds and grievances of their people in memorized verse. Welcome at any hearth, a bard's praise or mockery can make or unmake a man's standing among the clans.
description_summary: Singers and lore-keepers of the Dunlending clans, carrying genealogies, feuds and grievances in memorized verse; welcome at any hearth, their praise or mockery can make or unmake a man among the clans. Toward travellers: protected by custom and no fighters, but a powerful voice in the clans — and a keen source of local news and feud.

name: Snow-elves (Lossidil)
description: The Lossidil Snow-elves maintain Helloth, an enchanted ice city on the Landless Land, and patrol the polar wastes and Talath Uichel's far north, guarding Helecthil's radiance and intervening occasionally in mortal affairs.[file:39]
description_summary: Reclusive Snow-elves of the far polar wastes, keeping an enchanted ice-city and patrolling the frozen north, guarding an ancient radiance and rarely meddling in mortal affairs. Toward travellers: remote, seldom-seen guardians of the ice; aloof, but not hostile to those who mean no harm.

name: Iathrim and Laegrim
description: Sindar (Iathrim) and Green-elves (Laegrim) occupy Harlindon and southern Lindon, many as survivors of drowned Beleriand. Some resist living under Noldorin rule and eventually leave eastward to join Silvan realms, but others remain and shape Lindon's woodland culture and rituals described in the culture and lifestyle sections.[file:99]
description_summary: Sindar and Green-elves of Harlindon and southern Lindon — many of them survivors of drowned Beleriand — who shape the woodland culture of the western shores, some chafing under Noldorin rule. Toward travellers: woodland Elf-folk of the coast, courteous but reserved with outsiders.

name: Lindellim (Nandor)
description: Nandor Elves (Lindellim) and Mithrim-related northern Sindar houses contribute additional Elven lineages within Lindon. The appendices detail house histories, territories and military forces, showing how they occupy various parts of Forlindon, Harlindon and Rhûnlindon and how their demographic share grows relative to the Noldor over time.[file:99]
description_summary: Nandor Elves and northern Sindar houses of Lindon, holding parts of its forlands and shores — an old woodland Elven lineage among the western realms. Toward travellers: secretive forest-Elves, wary of strangers but not hostile without cause.

name: Angmarim Soldiers
description: Mannish soldiery of Angmar — hardened northern fighters in the service of the Witch-king, garrisoning his black towers and marching against the Dúnedain of the North. Grim and disciplined, they are the Iron Crown's mortal sword-arm.
description_summary: The mortal soldiery of Angmar — hardened northern fighters in the Witch-king's service, garrisoning his black towers and marching on the Dúnedain; grim and disciplined. Toward travellers: hostile servants of the Iron Crown who hunt and fight all enemies of Angmar.

name: Dwarves of the Blue Mountains
description: Dwarves inhabit halls and mines on the east side of the Blue Mountains, especially south of the Gulf of Lhûn; Thorin's Halls (Durin's Folk in exile) are located in this Lindon-adjacent zone. The introduction notes that even after all Elves depart in later Ages, Dwarves still dwell in their halls and keep mines in use.[file:99]
description_summary: Dwarves of the halls and mines on the eastern Blue Mountains, including Durin's Folk in exile at Thorin's Halls — enduring miners and smiths who keep their holds even after the Elves have gone. Toward travellers: industrious, proud and clannish; reserved with strangers but fair to honest dealers.

name: Goblin King of Gundabad
description: Title for the tyrannical overlord who rules Mount Gundabad and claims suzerainty over all Orc tribes of the mountains; his court in the spires is rife with intrigue, assassination, and revolt.
description_summary: The tyrant-overlord of Mount Gundabad who claims mastery over all the mountain orc-tribes, ruling a spire-court rife with intrigue, murder and revolt. Toward travellers: a cruel and dangerous orc-lord; to fall into his domain is to face capture or death.

name: Priests of Darkness
description: Orcish priests who preserve records of goblin deeds over two Ages and guard secret lore and rituals in hidden chambers, including tales of the coming from Morgoth's realm.
description_summary: Orcish priests who keep the records and dark lore of goblin-kind across the Ages, guarding secret rituals and tales of their coming from Morgoth's realm in hidden chambers. Toward travellers: malevolent keepers of evil lore, dangerous and fanatical within their hidden sanctums.

name: Helechoth
description: A land-focused Ystvät Talven sub-culture relying heavily on herd management and inland hunting, occupying central Talath Uichel and interacting with both Lossoth and Angmar's frontier forces.[file:39]
description_summary: A herding-and-hunting sub-folk of the far north, working inland herds across the cold uplands and dealing with both the snow-peoples and Angmar's frontier forces. Toward travellers: hardy northern herders, wary and self-contained on a bleak, contested frontier.

name: Umli (Half-dwarves)
description: The Umli live in the Far North of north-central Middle-earth, east of the Lossoth. They are a race of short folk who apparently came from an ancient union of Men and Dwarves. Legends call them Half-dwarves. Physical Character: Strong and compact, with large features and considerable facial hair. Males average 150 pounds; females 135 pounds. Ruddy skin and red hair, with penetrating pale blue eyes. Exceptional endurance in cold climes; average in temperate areas; poor in warm or hot climates. 4-5 feet. Lifespan: 100-200 years. +30 bonus versus cold/ice attacks and a -15 bonus versus heat/fire attacks. Need sleep but three hours a day. In the cold (below 35°) they halve any encumbrance penalties; above 90° they suffer double encumbrance penalties. Hunters, gatherers, and fisherman, they move from site to site depending on the season. Most reside in caves or underground settlements. Many mine, and the Umli are accomplished smiths and artisans.
description_summary: A short, stocky northern folk said to spring from an ancient union of Men and Dwarves — strong, ruddy, red-haired and bearded, supremely hardy in cold but ill-suited to heat, and long-lived. Toward travellers: tough, insular cold-country folk; gruff with strangers but no enemy to honest travellers.

name: Noldor (High Elves)
description: The Noldor are often called "High Elves" or "Deep Elves", ostensibly because they are considered to be the most noble of the Quendi in Middle-earth. In reality, they are so named because they are the only Elves living in Endor who have ever resided in the Blessed Realm of Aman across the sea. This exalted status is accentuated by their close ties with the Valar, a relationship which accounts for their unique cultural and linguistic roots. Other names for the Noldor include Deep-elves, Golodhrim or Gelydh (Sindarin labels), and Nómin (Edain label). Physical Character: Of all the Elves, the Noldor are the strongest and sturdiest of build, although they are still slimmer than Men. Most are dark haired and have greyish eyes which betray a proud looking manner. Descendants of the Fingolfin and Finarfin are often fair haired and blue eyed, for their blood contains Vanyar influences. They do not carry great burdens, but they are capable of traveling 16-20 hours a day. The Noldor do not sleep; instead they rest in a trance for 1-3 hours a day. Males average 6'7", females 6'3". Immortal and will only die due to violence or if they weary of life and lose the will to live. Cannot become sick or scarred and are virtually immune to disease. +20 bonus versus cold attacks. Unparalleled at understanding and working with crafts. +20 for Item Use rolls. Across Endor they are met in different guises: as lords, smiths and loremasters in Lindon, Rivendell and the ruined craft-halls of Eregion, and as wary wandering companies, scouts or exiles in the wild lands between.
description_summary: The "High Elves" — the noblest and most learned of Elf-kind in Middle-earth, the only ones who once dwelt in the Blessed Realm, close to the Valar and unmatched in craft, lore and war. Toward travellers: proud, ancient and formidable; gracious to the worthy, but carrying a weight of age and command that holds mortals in awe.

name: Dwarves of Khazad-dum
description: Durin's Folk in their ancestral seat of Khazad-dum (Moria); allied Dwarves who trade extensively with the Noldor of Eregion, exchanging ores and craft for Elven goods and knowledge, a friendship driven by shared interest in craft and learning.
description_summary: Durin's Folk in their ancestral glory at Moria, trading ore and craft with the Elves of Eregion in a famed friendship of shared learning. Toward travellers: proud master-smiths and miners at the height of their power; reserved but fair, and staunch friends to allies.

name: Merënyetsënyt
description: Ystvät Talven who dwell on moving icebergs and along the Bay of Forochel's shores, hunting whales, seals and walruses; they are stocky, short, fair-haired and remarkably resistant to cold and immersion, but suffer high mortality from the hazards of the sea.[file:39]
description_summary: A snow-people of the Bay of Forochel who live on drifting icebergs and frozen shores, hunting whales, seals and walrus — short, stocky, fair-haired and astonishingly cold-hardy, though many are lost to the sea. Toward travellers: hardy, isolated ice-hunters, reserved with outsiders but not hostile.

name: Noldorin mystics
description: An obscure order of Noldorin mystics maintains Evermist, a hidden valley on the Cape of Forochel that serves as a haven for the Free Peoples and base for resistance to the Witch-king of Angmar.[file:39]
description_summary: An obscure order of Noldorin mystics keeping a hidden valley-haven in the far north — a refuge for the Free Peoples and a base of resistance against the Witch-king. Toward travellers: secretive, wise and benevolent; a hidden help to the hunted who can find them.

name: Half-elves
description: Half-elves are a rare breed, the offspring of Men and Elves. While the Dúnedain, Corsairs, and Black Númenoreans have Elvish (and even Maia) blood, their connection to Elves lies in the remote past and they are not called Half-elves. True Half-elves have one Elven parent and one Mannish parent. Upon reaching adulthood, a Half-elf must decide whether to live as a mortal Man or an immortal Elf. Elrond of Rivendell chose the latter course, while his brother Elros - first King of Númenor - opted for mortality. Physical Character: Strong and slender, thinner than Men but stouter than Elves. They have thin, angular features. Males average 200 pounds, females 140 pounds. Fair, generally with brown or black hair and grey eyes. Considerable endurance. Half-elves need but 4 hours rest a day: sleep for mortals or meditation for the immortals. Males average 6'3", females 5'11". Mortal Half-elves live 250-500 years. Virtually immune to disease and natural illness. +5 bonus against cold attacks. Can see extremely well in the dark. Under moonlight or starlight they can see perfectly for 500'.
description_summary: The rare children of an Elf and a mortal, who at adulthood must choose to live as mortal Men or immortal Elves — strong, slender and gifted with the grace of both kindreds. Toward travellers: uncommon and remarkable, noble and self-possessed, bearing something of both worlds.

name: Horses Of Mordor
description: The forces of Evil, most notably the corrupted Maia Sauron, have bent Horses to their nefarious purposes. Sauron originally had his Nazgûl mounted on black Horses stolen as foals and thus accustomed to evil at an early age; these beasts were fortified by spells and potions to make them swift, tireless, and unafraid of things that would terrify a normal steed. By the end of the Third Age the Dark Lord had succeeded in reshaping some of the stolen foals: enlarged and strengthened, given carnivorous foreteeth, fiery night-sighted eyes, and steaming, smoking breath, their faces became skull-like and their hooves like polished iron. One such was the Mount of the Mouth of Sauron, which bore its master to the parley at the Black Gate during the War of the Ring; exceedingly large and powerful, it could run almost as fast as a Meara, with sharp-edged hooves and saw-like jaws that penetrated armor. Its greatest weapon was its foul steam, akin to the Nazgûl's Black Breath (a 30-foot range, affecting any target within a 5-foot radius). Note: this creature can be characterized as a monster; see Section 6.0 regarding Evil Horses.
description_summary: Steeds bent to evil — black horses stolen as foals and raised to darkness, fortified by spell and potion to be swift, tireless and fearless; the worst reshaped into enlarged, carnivorous, fiery-eyed monsters. Toward travellers: war-mounts of the Enemy's servants; the reshaped kind are predatory horrors, not natural beasts.

name: Great Falcons Of Mirkwood
description: Rarely seen despite their huge size, Great Falcons reside in the remoter and higher reaches of the Forest of Mirkwood (actually most reside in the Emyn-nu-Fuin, the "Mountains of Mirkwood"). They are almost untameable, probably due to the difficulty of hooding and confining such a huge bird, even as an eyas (fledgling). Great Falcons are surprisingly maneuverable for such large birds, as is necessary for any winged beast that would live in the dense forest. Their light build makes them unable to carry more than around 70 lbs, so they make poor mounts.
description_summary: Huge but rarely-seen falcons of the high Mountains of Mirkwood — surprisingly agile for their size to live in dense forest, and all but untameable. Toward travellers: shy of people and seldom seen; a magnificent, wary hunter of the forest heights.

name: Red Eagles
description: The Red Eagle dominates the skies of the East. Smaller relatives of the Great Eagles, who are rare in eastern Endor, these magnificent predators are relatively numerous and pose a threat to travellers in the Red Mountains and Mountains of the Wind. Fortunately, though, they usually prefer to hunt goats, sheep, or wild horses. Red Eagles are known for the deep red hue that accents the tips of their gold-colored plumage. They have scarlet beaks.
description_summary: Bold red-tipped, gold-plumed eagles that rule the eastern skies — smaller kin of the Great Eagles but numerous, usually hunting goats, sheep and wild horses, and a real threat to travellers in their mountains. Toward travellers: aggressive for an eagle; they may stoop on people crossing their high ranges.

name: Great Green Pheasants
description: The Great Green Pheasant is a handsome bird. Females and their immature offspring sport lustrous bronze-green plumage. Emerald green feathers and long, trailing tail plumes adorn the male. The cocks are famous for their ferocious duels over potential mates: they rush at each other fiercely, broadsiding their rivals in clouds of emerald green down, until one or the other retreats, exhausted and bedraggled. Great Green Pheasants, like the Grass Grouse, are considered tasty, but this larger bird must be hung before eating, as it is tougher (in every way) than the passive Grass Grouse.
description_summary: Handsome ground-birds — the cocks emerald-feathered with long trailing plumes, famed for fierce shoving-duels over mates; tasty but tough, needing to be hung before eating. Toward travellers: harmless; sought as game.

name: Great Bats
description: Seldom-seen, the grey and purple Great Bat is found in remoter parts of Mirkwood. Little is known of its habits. It eats night-flying birds (e.g., Owls), large spiders, and various rodents, although larger individuals have been known to fell a small bear. The Great Bat sucks blood (always a tempting notion to those who dislike bats, even the small insectivorous kind) from its victims, and feeds on the softer flesh of its prey.
description_summary: A seldom-seen grey-and-purple bat of remote Mirkwood that hunts night-birds, large spiders and rodents — the larger ones able to fell a small bear — and sucks the blood and soft flesh of its prey. Toward travellers: a rare but dangerous blood-drinker of the deep forest night; the great ones can threaten a lone sleeper.

name: Mordor Cattle
description: Hardy, ill-tempered cattle bred in the grey fields of Nurn to feed the armies of Mordor. Tended by slaves under the lash, they are lean, half-wild beasts suited to the harsh land that raises them.
description_summary: Hardy, ill-tempered cattle bred in the grey fields of Nurn to feed Mordor's armies — lean, half-wild beasts tended by slaves under the lash and suited to their harsh land. Toward travellers: bad-tempered and half-wild; no predator, but a sign one is deep in the Enemy's country.

name: Sea Eagles
description: Smaller, swifter kinsmen of the fierce Red Eagles, Sea Eagles live along the coasts of the Romenear (Q. "East Sea") and the eastern Haragaer (S. "South Sea"). There, they hunt sea mammals, large fish that swim near the surface, and even sea turtles. The bane of Water Drakes, Sea Eagles are often worshipped as saviors by the seafaring tribes of the remote eastern isles. Sea Eagles have yellowish-gold underplumage and blue-green feathers on the tops of their backs and wings. They have golden beaks.
description_summary: Swift coastal eagles of the eastern seas — smaller kin of the Red Eagles — hunting sea-mammals, surface fish and even sea-turtles, bane of water-drakes and worshipped as saviours by some island tribes; gold-and-blue-green plumed. Toward travellers: little threat to people, a majestic hunter of the coasts and a good omen to seafarers.

name: Watchers In The Water
description: Freshwater Kraken that normally reside in the pools of the Underdeeps. One emerged into the Sirannon by the West Gate of Moria, damming it into a gloomy lake and, driven by Sauron's hatred, preying on Dwarves and Orcs before it nearly seized Frodo as the Fellowship entered Moria in T.A. 3019.
description_summary: A monstrous freshwater kraken of the lightless deeps — many-tentacled and immense; one lurks in the dammed lake before Moria's West Gate, set by Sauron's hatred to seize prey from the water's edge. Toward travellers: a lethal ambush from the water — long arms that snatch the unwary into the dark depths.

name: Sauron's Armies
description: Huge armies of Orcs, Olog-hai (trolls), and Easterling/Haradrim auxiliaries garrisoning the black towers.
description_summary: The vast hosts of Mordor — orcs, Olog-hai trolls and Easterling and Haradrim auxiliaries — garrisoning the black towers and marching to war. Toward travellers: overwhelming and merciless; to be caught by a marching host of the Enemy is doom.

name: Marsh Crocodiles
description: Swift but usually inactive, the Marsh, or "Fish-eating," Crocodiles are common in the wetter, warmer parts of Endor. They are inoffensive when let alone, but can be quite deadly when surprised (or hungry). Black, with green stripes and light blue undersides, they are colorful carnivores. Their diet consists of large mammals and birds who enter or come to the edge of the water. After grabbing its prey in its long, powerful snout, the Marsh Crocodile drowns the victim and then tears the flaccid body apart with twisting bites. It spends its nights in the water and basks on shore during the day, feeding on average once every two days, and swallows rocks for ballast.
description_summary: Swift, colourful crocodiles (black with green stripes) of warm wetlands — usually idle and inoffensive if left alone, but deadly when surprised or hungry, seizing prey at the water's edge, drowning it and tearing it apart. Toward travellers: a hidden killer along warm marsh-banks; safe at a distance, lethal up close.

name: Wels
description: A species of giant catfish, the Wels is an ugly brute. They are usually up to 4-5' long, but sometimes reach 10'. A larger Wels weighs nearly 700 lbs. It has a rounded body with a flattened head and a wide, horizontal mouth, around which are two long sensory barbels and four short ones. Wels are known to swallow prey whole and will sometimes bash boats to see what falls out.
description_summary: A giant catfish — an ugly, flat-headed brute up to ten feet and hundreds of pounds, with long sensory whiskers, swallowing prey whole and sometimes battering boats to see what spills out. Toward travellers: little threat to a person, but its size can startle and even upset a small boat.

name: Fishing Cats
description: Aquatic cats most common in the marshes along the eastern shores of the Sea of Rhûn and in the Wetwang (Nindalf) in Ithilien. With no dislike of water and semi-webbed paws, they swim well and flip fish from the water, supplementing their diet with birds, shellfish and amphibians. Mottled grey-brown fur hides them among reeds; kittens can be tamed but stay one-person pets.
description_summary: Water-loving cats of the eastern marshes, with semi-webbed paws — strong swimmers that flip fish from the water and also take birds, shellfish and frogs, hiding mottled among the reeds; kittens can be tamed as one-person pets. Toward travellers: shy and water-bound, no real threat to people.

name: Barrow Owls
description: The Barrow Owl is a small, rare Owl which has taken to living in the open barrows and abandoned gopher tunnels of Eriador (especially in northern Cardolan). Nocturnal and exceedingly specialized, they are virtually blind when confronted with bright light (e.g., in day- or torchlight). This is important, for they startle easily and swarm like Bees when confronted with a potential foe. They carry numerous diseases, including rabies and lycanthresis, to which they are immune. (Normally 2nd-3rd level, the latter disease is a severe psychiatric infliction that affects victims in 110 days and leaves them with the false belief that they are wild animals.)
description_summary: A small, rare owl that nests in open barrows and old burrows of Eriador — nearly blind in bright light, easily startled, swarming like bees when threatened, and carrying many diseases to which it is immune. Toward travellers: harmless singly, but a startled colony in a barrow can swarm and is foul with sickness.

name: Wargs
description: Great evil wolves of the North, larger and more cunning than common wolves and bound in malice to the Orcs, whom they often bear into battle. They hunt in savage packs, share a crude speech of their own, and gather at the Enemy's call.
description_summary: Great evil wolves of the North — larger and more cunning than common wolves, bound in malice to the orcs they often bear into battle; they hunt in savage packs, share a crude speech and gather at the Enemy's call. Toward travellers: a deadly, intelligent pack-hunter and servant of darkness, a grave danger on wild roads, above all by night.

name: Beacon
description: A signal-beacon set on hill or tower, lit in time of war or need so that warning may leap from height to height across the land. A landmark by day and a summons by night.
description_summary: A signal-beacon set on hill or tower, lit in war or need so that warning may leap from height to height across the land — a landmark by day, a summons by night. Toward travellers: harmless in itself; a sign of watch and warding, and word that something is afoot when it burns.

name: Short-Eared Owls
description: A rare day-hunting Owl, the Short-eared Owl is quite small. Like many other Endorian birds, however, it is an intelligent and perceptive creature. They were taught by the Elves to speak and have their own language. One who is close to Nature can derive helpful information from them.
description_summary: A small, rare day-hunting owl — intelligent and perceptive, taught speech by the Elves and keeping its own tongue; those close to nature can learn much from it. Toward travellers: harmless and wise, a possible source of knowledge to one who can speak with beasts.

name: Trusavi
description: An arboreal creature, the Trusa (P. "Leaf Frog"; pl. "Trusavi") is adapted for gliding from tree to tree. It has a broad head, a long slim body, and elongated limbs with webbed feet. Flaps of skin fringe the forelimbs and heels, permitting the creature to extend its body and create a bizarre but effective set of "wings." This bright red and blue frog grows no larger than about 3 inches in length, but it is among the deadliest animals in the humid woodlands of southeastern Middle-earth. If touched, the spines on the back of the Trusa will inject a strong (lvl 4) poison which acts in 1-10 rds. A victim who fails his RR by 21 or more dies, while one who fails by 11-20 becomes blind and falls into a coma; a RR failure of 01-10 leaves the victim blind in 1-2 eyes.
description_summary: A tiny gliding tree-frog of southeastern rainforest — bright red and blue, with skin-flaps that let it sail between trees — and among the deadliest creatures of those woods, its back-spines injecting a lethal poison at a touch. Toward travellers: small and beautiful but lethal, never to be handled.

name: Cunara
description: The most common rodent in Cardolan, the Cunaura (Du. "Gophers") of Eriador resemble big stout Rats and live mainly on grass roots. On the plains they are a major nuisance because they live in large colonies of interlocking burrows. Their holes rise up about a foot from ground level but their tunnels run very close to the surface and cannot be easily detected by Sheep, Horses, or Men (leading to many broken legs).
description_summary: The common gopher-like rodent of Cardolan — stout, rat-like grass-root eaters living in vast colonies of shallow burrows whose hidden tunnels can snap the leg of a horse or a man. Toward travellers: harmless in itself, but their burrow-riddled ground is a real hazard to mounts.

name: Aurych
description: The Auroch (pl. "Aurych") is a domesticated breed of the wild cattle or Kine that roam Rhovanion (Wilderland). They vary in color, but most have medium length, dark brown fur on their bodies, and shorter, whiter fur on their legs and foreheads. Males have larger, curving, conical horns. Like all Kine, Aurych migrate with the seasons and tend to wander wide areas whenever breeding.
description_summary: Domesticated cattle of the Wilderland — dark-brown bodied with paler legs and brows, the bulls bearing large curving horns; they migrate with the seasons and roam widely. Toward travellers: ordinary herd-cattle, harmless unless a bull is provoked.

name: Egils Vipers
description: A huge, poisonous, predatory serpent, Egil's Viper is one of the strangest hazards of the plain of Talath Harroch, south of Mirkwood. It is said that these remarkable snakes were driven out of other areas of Rhovanion into the Talath Harroch, but this seems unlikely at best. They prey upon eggs, rodents, young animals, and (in the case of the largest Vipers) Men and Kine. Of a yellowish-brown color that blends well with the soil and grasses of the plain, Egil's Viper hunts in the late afternoon or early evening. It may spit its venom into a victim's eyes from a distance of up to thirty feet, or it may bite and inject venom in the normal manner. The Viper's fangs reach up to three inches in length and can penetrate leather and, in some cases, even chain armor.
description_summary: A huge predatory viper of the plains south of Mirkwood, yellow-brown to match the soil, hunting in the evening for rodents, young beasts and (the largest) even Men and cattle — and able to spit its venom. Toward travellers: a serious danger on the open plain, striking or spitting poison from hiding.

name: Demon Whales
description: Huge carnivorous white or grey creatures of the Ice Bay of Forochel, with five-inch steel-tearing teeth that can swallow a man whole (survival in the acidic stomach being unlikely). They often shatter whale-boats or overturn larger vessels.
description_summary: Huge carnivorous whales of the icy northern bay, white or grey, with five-inch teeth that shear steel and jaws that can swallow a man whole; they shatter boats and overturn ships. Toward travellers: catastrophic to any vessel — a leviathan that wrecks ships and devours the crew.

name: Slow-Fangs
description: The Slow-fang is one of the most dangerous and dominant creatures in the rain forests of southern Endor. Large (9' long) and exceptionally strong, it lives in trees and hunts large mammals and smaller reptiles. Its body coloration varies with the surface it happens to cross, although it is usually brownish and has mottled green stripes. They have friction pads on their feet and specialized scales that enable them to stick to vertical surfaces for indefinite periods, making them naturally agile tree-climbers; this impairs their movement on the jungle floor, where they are awkward runners. Endowed with a strong, 6' long prehensile tongue that acts like a whip or bola, they can strike a foe quickly. Then they bite the immobilized victim with a pair of 7" fangs, injecting a mild (2nd lvl) muscle poison; this disables the prey long enough for the Slow-fang to slowly discard any inedible trappings (e.g., clothing) before swallowing the meal in one long gulp.
description_summary: A huge (nine-foot) tree-dwelling predator of southern rainforest, immensely strong and colour-shifting to match its surroundings, with friction-padded feet that let it cling to any vertical surface — a deadly, agile climber that hunts large mammals. Toward travellers: a dominant ambush hunter of the canopy, lethal and very hard to see.

name: Umakavi
description: A small tree frog from the rainforests of the Mûmakan in the south of Middle-earth, the Umak (pl. "Umakavi") is notable for the potent contact poison exuded by its colorful skin. Bright lime green and streaked with yellow and orange, the Umak Tree Frog is noticeable, but it is so rare that it is still nearly impossible to spot. The bright coloring warns away animal and bird predators who would be poisoned upon grabbing the frog in their beaks or mouths, but merely makes things easier for clever human or Elf hunters who come armed with nets on long handles. The poison is tenth level in contact with skin; unlucky individuals are killed instantly (upon failing a RR), while the more fortunate are plunged into a coma until awakened by some form of antidote.
description_summary: A tiny, vivid lime-green tree-frog of the far-southern rainforest, streaked yellow and orange, exuding a potent contact poison from its skin; rare and hard to find despite its bright warning colours. Toward travellers: deadly to touch — hunters take it only with nets, never bare hands.

name: Stetanavi
description: In Apysaic, Stetan means "mischievous." It applies perfectly to this otter-like creature. The beast's body is as large as a big dog, and it is as friendly and playful as the most lively of puppies. It has very short legs, though, so no one would mistake it for a hound. The Stetan's name was earned from the Haradrim who journeyed into the wood, worked a long day gathering herbs, then saw their sacks and parcels playfully torn apart just before they returned home. Only their overpowering air of pure innocence keeps Stetanavi from being killed on sight by most men who glean their living from the wood. As it is, they are usually viewed as a sign of long-term good luck even if they are a short-term nuisance. There have been attempts to tame the friendly beasts, but Stetanavi taken into captivity invariably die shortly thereafter, an occurrence usually blamed on loneliness for their own kind. It is possible to overcome this through the use of a Familiar spell, though having such a puckish creature as a familiar would certainly have its drawbacks.
description_summary: A dog-sized, otter-like creature of the southern woods — short-legged, irrepressibly friendly and playful as a puppy, infamous for cheerfully tearing open foragers' packs; its sheer innocence is what keeps it from being killed. Toward travellers: utterly harmless and endearing, a mischievous pest rather than a danger.

name: Sand Devils
description: Tortured spirits of Men who died in desert sandstorms, lingering as whirlwind-like beings that roam the open wastes searching for victims, whom they envelop within their swirling form.
description_summary: The tortured spirits of men lost in desert sandstorms, lingering as whirlwind-like beings that roam the wastes hunting victims to engulf within their swirling form. Toward travellers: a deadly haunt of the open desert — a living sandstorm that seeks out and smothers the lone wanderer.

name: Death Shrews
description: Tiny, mild-looking shrews with soft white fur and blue-grey bellies that carry the dread Blue Hand disease: victims turn bluish, break out in boils, bleed from nose and ears, and many are crippled or kill themselves. The shrews are merely immune carriers. Superb climbers that avoid most encounters, but when cornered they bite exposed skin viciously and secrete a foul spray; their spines can bear a Man's weight.
description_summary: Tiny, harmless-looking white shrews that carry a dread plague — victims turn blue, break out in boils and bleed — though the shrews themselves are merely immune carriers; superb climbers that avoid notice but bite viciously and spray foulness when cornered. Toward travellers: deceptively deadly, not by tooth but by the sickness they carry; never to be touched.

name: Dire Wolves
description: Large cousin (S. "Gothraug") of the Timber Wolf, found all over northern Middle-earth in family groups (2-11) or packs (10-60). Intelligent, social and mated for life, settling disputes without bloodshed and cooperating in the hunt; unlike common wolves they have little fear of the Free Peoples and will hunt Men, Elves or Dwarves. Up to 150 lbs, 30 mph, leaping 6 ft, with tremendous nightvision.
description_summary: Large, intelligent cousins of the timber wolf across the North, deeply social and mated for life, hunting in cooperative packs; unlike common wolves they little fear the Free Peoples and will hunt Men, Elves or Dwarves. Toward travellers: a bold, clever and dangerous pack-hunter unafraid of people, a real threat especially in numbers.

name: Astabanhéli
description: The Astabanhe or Giant Pronghorn is an antelope-like creature found mainly in the Pinnath Ceren (S. "Red Fells") and the Girithlin highlands of Eriador. They stand about 4' tall and are dull yellow in color. While renowned for their loud white rump, their short black horns give the Astabanhéli their name. Pronghorns travel in small herds (10-100) and are inquisitive, but they are quick to flee. Movements of their white tail fur signal danger when there is danger or when they have taken to flight. They can travel at speeds of up to forty miles per hour and can swim quite well.
description_summary: A giant pronghorn antelope of the Eriadoran highlands — about four feet tall, dull yellow with short black horns and a conspicuous white rump — travelling in inquisitive but skittish herds and bolting at great speed when alarmed. Toward travellers: harmless; a swift, wary grazer that flashes its white tail and flees.

name: Cats
description: A handsome, long-legged hunter of the plains of Calenardhon, the Grass Cat is now quite rare. Men have killed or domesticated most of its prey and actively hunt the Cats for fear of losing Sheep, Goats, or Kine. A Grass Cat has a thick, short, buff-colored fur that easily absorbs grass pollen in the summer months, giving it an eerie and unnatural yellow-green coloration. The Cat hunts by leaping upon the backs of its prey, digging its long claws into the victim's throat and neck. Even if its victim is not killed by the severing of some vital vein or nerve, it will run itself into a stupor or even to death. There have been cases of Grass Cats killing Men in this same manner. When in heat, courting, or fighting. Grass Cats give off highpitched, raucous shrieks that are terrifying to hear, especially during the long winter nights.
probability_by_region: [{"region": "Central Anduin", "probability": 1}, {"region": "Central Misty Mountains", "probability": 1}, {"region": "Druadan Forest", "probability": 3}, {"region": "Nan Anduin", "probability": 2}, {"region": "North Misty Mountains", "probability": 1}, {"region": "Rohan", "probability": 3}, {"region": "Ronen-in-Anduin", "probability": 2}, {"region": "Westmarch", "probability": 2}, {"region": "Wold", "probability": 1}]
description_summary: The wild and half-tame cats of farm, town and field — small hunters of mice, rats and birds, kept by folk as mousers or gone feral about settlements and ruins. Toward travellers: harmless and aloof; a barn-cat by the hearth, or a wary shape slipping through a ruin.

name: Cliff Lions
description: Snowy-white lion (Q. "Rálimba") of southwestern Middle-earth, its core territory around the Bay of Drel and the lower reaches of the Yellow Mountains near Lake Aeluin. Solitary and shy of virtually sheer terrain; active morning and evening, hunting goats, sheep, rodents and carrion, striking swiftly at the nape of its prey.
description_summary: A snowy-white lion of southwestern Middle-earth, solitary and shy, keeping to near-sheer terrain and hunting at dawn and dusk, striking swiftly at the nape of goats, sheep and small game. Toward travellers: avoids people, but a powerful ambush killer of the crags if surprised.

name: Blue Bears
description: Monstrous, bluish-grey bear (Q. "Megliluine") found only on the southern slopes of the White Mountains, especially Lamedon (the Morthond Valley) in Gondor. Has a constant innate Presence ability, sensing living beings up to 50 ft. Often attacks without provocation; usually solitary but will hunt in family groups, defending territory even against Men and Elves.
description_summary: A monstrous bluish-grey bear of the southern White Mountains that can sense living things nearby by instinct and often attacks without provocation, fiercely territorial even against Men and Elves. Toward travellers: unusually aggressive — a bear that may hunt or charge people unprovoked, and very dangerous.

name: Merisc Tyka
description: Wild dog (pl. Merisc Tyka) found throughout Rhovanion, Rhun and the lands of the Kykurian Kyn, related to the feral dogs of the Talath Harroch south of Mirkwood. Frequenting fens and moorlands in small packs, it fishes, scavenges carrion and attacks small mammals, and will prey on weak, stranded or sickly Men. It is afraid of fire and easily driven off with torchlight; its dappled yellowish-tan and brownish-grey coat grants a +40 Hiding bonus.
description_summary: Wild dogs of the eastern fens and moors, ranging in small packs to fish, scavenge and take small game — and to prey on weak, stranded or sickly Men; easily driven off with fire and torchlight. Toward travellers: a danger mainly to the lone and the weak, readily scared off with flame.

name: Sheep Hounds
description: Popular among shepherds throughout Middle-earth, the favourite being the large, short-muzzled, long-haired Sheep Hound (Q. Huoton). It endures harsh weather and performs even when fatigued, and its size, stealth and speed ward off predators; very loyal, intelligent and fierce, a collared hound is a match for two or three Wolves. The Dunedain also use them for hunting and as guard dogs, though war-dog experiments failed; a smaller breed is kept as a pet for women and children.
description_summary: The big, shaggy herding-dogs favoured by shepherds across Middle-earth — hardy, loyal, intelligent and fierce, a single collared hound a match for two or three wolves, also used by the Dúnedain for hunting and guarding. Toward travellers: friendly to their masters and guests, but formidable guardians that will face down a wolf or a thief.

name: Red Foxes Of Calenardhon
description: Unlike the common variety, the Red Fox of Calenardhon has truly red rather than rust-coloured fur, making it a prized fur target. Fortunately it is also cleverer than the average fox, wily about avoiding Men and traps, and rarely stumbles into death, so it must be actively hunted. A canny thief, it steals hens, rabbits and small bright objects, and unlike most carnivores enjoys some colour vision.
description_summary: A truly red-furred, prized fox of the Rohan plains — cleverer than the common kind, wily about Men and traps, a canny thief of hens, rabbits and small bright things, and (rare among beasts) able to see some colour. Toward travellers: harmless and elusive, a sly raider of coops rather than any danger.

name: Marsh Mastiffs
description: Tall, domesticated dogs found wherever the Dunedain have resided or held extensive commercial links, with long legs and short wiry coats of dark brown or russet. Well-liked by shepherds, herders and hunters, they are sure-footed, good swimmers, keen-nosed, fearless and loyal, and are often fitted with collars and even leather coats.
description_summary: Tall, long-legged domestic dogs of Dúnedain lands, with short wiry russet coats — sure-footed, strong swimmers, keen-nosed, fearless and loyal, favoured by shepherds, herders and hunters. Toward travellers: loyal working dogs, friendly with their folk but staunch guardians against intruders.

name: Gích
description: Large fox (pl. "Gích") found throughout the foothills of the Misty Mountains and the chief small predator of Rhudaur. Its name means "changer": black in summer, white in winter, and striped while moulting, a phase the superstitious Rhudaurans hold sacrosanct and believe weapon-proof. Eats small mammals, eggs, ripe fruit and carrion; its fur is marketable but not especially valuable.
description_summary: A large fox of the Misty Mountain foothills and chief small predator of Rhudaur — named "changer" for its coat (black in summer, white in winter, striped while moulting), which the superstitious hold sacred and weapon-proof; it eats small game, fruit and carrion. Toward travellers: harmless to people, a wary hunter steeped in local superstition.

name: Dunmen's Dogs
description: Squat dogs resembling a large, fat toy hound, kept only by the tribal Dunlendings. Sometimes used as guards or for pack-hunting, at which they are marginally capable; most Dunmen's Dogs end up in the stewpot.
description_summary: Squat, fat little hounds kept only by the Dunlendings, used after a fashion as guards or pack-hunters but poor at both — and as often as not ending in the cooking-pot. Toward travellers: harmless and unimpressive, a sign of a Dunlending camp more than any threat.

name: The Majmun
description: Hordes of lively monkeys living among the trees of the forest called "Gaj" in western Far Harad, about three feet head to tail and flitting through branches like squirrels. Less intelligent than their more developed relatives but endearing to Men who visit the Gaj, they are neither noisy nor unclean and act tame around typical Haradan, though shyer with outlandishly dressed folk; great swarms haunt the temple grounds so respectfully the faithful insist they worship there. Their attractive gold-and-black fur is speckled with light that glows in the dark, and tales tell of foreign pelt-trappers who met ghastly ends, swarmed by the chattering beasts.
description_summary: Lively, tree-flitting monkeys of a Far Harad forest, about three feet long, endearing and tame around the local folk but shyer of strangely-dressed outsiders; great troops haunt a temple so respectfully the faithful say they worship there. Toward travellers: harmless and curious, though wary of foreign-looking visitors.

name: Undarlaif
description: Large black jackals (Rh. Leavings) that entered the Talath Harroch, the southern Rhovanion plain below Mirkwood, during the Great Plague of T.A. 1636-1637, having migrated from the East; they now range from the western flanks of the Orocarni to the eastern bank of the Anduin. Their reddish ears and eerie blue eyes give an innocent guise that hides sly, pitiless ways. Hunting at night in coordinated packs of 4-20, they encircle prey, decoy the strongest defenders and close on the choicest victims, scavenging carrion in lean times.
description_summary: Large black jackals with reddish ears and eerie blue eyes that give an innocent look hiding sly, pitiless ways — night-hunters in coordinated packs that encircle prey, decoy its defenders and pick off the choicest victims, scavenging in lean times. Toward travellers: a cunning, dangerous pack-predator of the night, best met with fire and numbers.

name: Shaking Asps
description: This small, gray snake has a distinctive ruff of loose scales that give off a faint, odd rattling sound when the serpent is startled or about to strike. Its potent venom (level 8) causes massive internal hemorrhage, causing tremendous (24-60 hits) shock and often (with 26+ RR failure) death. The Shaking Asp is a native of Rhudaur, the region in eastern Eriador between the Mitheithel (Hoarwell) and Bruinen (Loudwater) Rivers.
description_summary: A small grey snake with a ruff of loose scales that gives a faint rattling sound when startled, native to Rhudaur; its potent venom causes massive internal bleeding and often death. Toward travellers: a deadly bite for so small a snake — heed the dry rattle and give it room.

name: Uncavi
description: Large cat (the Unca) of the hilly woodlands of Far Harad, often the only large mammal in its ecosystem and rather smaller than the Leopard it resembles. Its pelt is splotched in shades of brown for perfect camouflage as it prowls the overgrown jungle floor or hides in low branches; since most local prey is small and quick it spends much time chasing through the brush, though its sturdy build lets it take larger game. It avoids the deadly Petla, hunts the playful Stetan, and will take Men by surprise.
description_summary: A leopard-like cat of the Far Harad woodlands, smaller than a true leopard and splotched brown for perfect jungle camouflage — prowling the undergrowth and low branches for mostly small, swift prey but able to bring down larger, and able to take a man by surprise. Toward travellers: a stealthy ambush hunter of the jungle that may strike the unwary.

name: Vuk
description: Wild hill dog of the arid and semi-arid hills of southern Endor, abundant in Far Harad, Chy, Vaag and Gaathgykarkan and ranging east to Horl. It resembles a wolf but with a larger, squarer snout and a mottled dark-tan coat spotted grey and brown for camouflage. With a wolf's strength and a jackal's pride it is resilient, hunting in packs and turning to carrion when game is scarce; rather than ambush it runs prey to exhaustion, circling and giving off yelping barks until even the region's tough wild goats fall.
description_summary: A wolf-like wild hill-dog of the arid south, mottled tan and grey for camouflage, with a wolf's strength and a jackal's boldness — pack-hunters that run prey to exhaustion, circling and yelping until even tough wild goats fall, turning to carrion when game is scarce. Toward travellers: a persistent, dangerous pack-predator of dry country.

name: Mûmakil
description: The huge, fearsome, and majestic Mûmak (W. "Oliphaunt"; P. "Tembo"; pl. "Tembovi") is a close relative of the Andamunda (Elephant) and is often referred to as the Gorsandamunda (Q. "Giant Elephant"). It is the "lord" of Endor's southern wilds, growing up to thirty feet in length and standing up to 17 feet in height at the shoulder, with few foes. Fortunately for their rivals, Mûmakil are vegetarians; they rest in the midday heat and roam in search of nutritious roots, leaves, shoots, twigs, and fruits, using their agile trunks and huge tusks (which grow as long as 7'). These social beasts travel in herds and adapt (albeit reluctantly) to domestication. When aroused, they can be deadly fighters; both the Úsakani and the Mûmakani have long prized them as mounts for war, and the Haradrim build great fighting towers to place on their backs, decorating the legs and tusks. The thick hides of Mûmakil make them almost invulnerable to arrows except for their eyes, but when blinded they often run mad with pain, trampling and destroying everything in their paths.
description_summary: The Oliphaunt — a colossal relative of the elephant, "lord" of the southern wilds, up to thirty feet long and seventeen high at the shoulder, with huge tusks and an agile trunk; a vegetarian with few natural foes, ridden to war by southern peoples. Toward travellers: peaceable while grazing, but a charging or war-driven Mûmak is an unstoppable, earth-shaking force.

name: Uvag-Aak
description: Blood-drinking apes of the Tanturak jungle of the Mumakan in southernmost Endor, who defeated the Men and Elves that once sought to settle the region. Intensely territorial, they will not leave their jungle unless food becomes scarce; blood is their primary and preferred nutrition, though they eat flesh in lean times. Fiercely protective of their own, they exact horrible vengeance on any who kill one of their kind, leaving the blood-drained corpses of their victims where other would-be exterminators are sure to see them.
description_summary: Blood-drinking apes of a far-southern jungle who once destroyed the Men and Elves that tried to settle there — fiercely territorial, living chiefly on blood, and taking horrible vengeance on any who kill one of their own, leaving drained corpses on display. Toward travellers: a deadly, vengeful jungle-dweller; to enter their forest or harm one is to invite slaughter.

name: Vodyanoi
description: Aquatic carnivore and relative of the Giant Hellbender, resembling a giant black frog and preferring the flesh of people. It lurks in dark pools, dragging prey underwater to drown or swallowing it whole by dislocating its jaw like a snake; its huge eyes can hypnotise and distract its victims (5th-level RR).
description_summary: A man-eating water-beast like a giant black frog that lurks in dark pools, dragging prey under to drown or swallowing it whole by unhinging its jaw; its huge eyes can hypnotise and bewilder a victim. Toward travellers: a hidden killer of dark waters whose hypnotic gaze and sudden lunge make pools and meres treacherous.

name: Red Jaws
description: Ugly, scaleless cavern fish especially common in Moria, with eyes set on top of its head and gaping shark-like jaws of up to seven rows of teeth (harmless to Dwarves). Colorless save for a bright red jaw and luminescent fins that flick brilliantly in dark water; it swallows a dog or goat whole or devours a Dwarf's leg in seconds.
description_summary: An ugly, scaleless cavern-fish common in Moria — eyes set atop its head and gaping shark-like jaws of many rows of teeth, colourless but for a bright red jaw and luminous flicking fins; it can swallow a goat whole or take off a leg in seconds. Toward travellers: a lethal lurker of dark underground water, where a single bite can maim or kill.

name: Zûrafy
description: The Zûrafy (V. "Tallnecks"; sing. "Zûrafa") of Núrad, northern Chey, and the Talathrant Valley resemble giraffes with long, spikelike horns. They are related to Deer and feed on the foliage, fruit, and buds found in the upper part of trees or tall scrub; in harsher locales the Zûrafa will stoop to eat grasses, and herds have been known to devastate crops. The Núriags, Variags, and Chey use the Zûrafy as mounts, for they are swift, fearless, and exceedingly fierce combatants. Because of their limited numbers and the difficulty in capturing and domesticating them, only small numbers have been employed outside the circles of royalty. Most Zûrafy have a whitish or light tan coat and brown-and-white striped legs; their horns are covered with short white hair which they never shed. The rare, omnivorous, nocturnal Variag Zûrafa is jet black.
description_summary: Giraffe-like beasts of the far southeast with long spike-like horns, browsing the tops of trees and scrub and stooping to grass or crops in hard country — swift, fearless and exceedingly fierce in a fight, ridden as war-mounts by some eastern peoples. Toward travellers: peaceable browsers in the wild, but formidable and aggressive when ridden to war.

name: Calenardhon Ruins
description: The ruins of Calenardhon, the grassland province of Gondor before it became Rohan — abandoned forts, road-stations and steadings standing empty on the plains, slowly reclaimed by grass and weather.
description_summary: The ruins of Calenardhon — Gondor's grassland province before it became Rohan — abandoned forts, road-stations and steadings standing empty on the plains, slowly swallowed by grass and weather. Toward travellers: a melancholy open ruin; shelter of a sort, but a haunt of beasts and brigands as much as a refuge.

name: Spotted Lions
description: Named for the black spots on its almost-white underbelly above a burnt-orange coat, the Spotted Lion is now quite rare from hunting but still ranges throughout Cardolan. Not evil but extremely cunning and speechless, its old, bitter feud with the wolves has hastened its decline alongside the efforts of Men. It lives in small family groups, lairs in trees and caves, prefers to hunt Fiara (sheep will do), and will kill men in self-defence.
description_summary: A rare, cunning lion of Cardolan — burnt-orange above with a spotted near-white belly — living in small family groups, lairing in trees and caves, hunting deer and sheep, locked in an old feud with the wolves, and killing men only in self-defence. Toward travellers: shy of people and not aggressive, but a powerful predator that will fight if cornered.

name: Fiara
description: Thriving on the scant grasses of south-central Eriador, the Fiara is the most common wild herbivore in Cardolan and Minhiriath. They keep to small groups of four or five does and a buck and range all over the region. Extremely timid, though, they shy away from settled areas and seem much rarer than their real numbers would dictate. It is fortunate that these small beasts are prolific, for no creature in northwestern Endor is hunted as often as the Fiara. Eriadoran hunters have long relied on the Fiara as a food staple, and cooks from Bree to Tharbad create dozens of dishes using this tasty Deer meat.
description_summary: A small, extremely timid wild roe-deer of south-central Eriador — the commonest wild herbivore of Cardolan and Minhiriath, keeping to small groups and shunning settled land; the most hunted game of the North. Toward travellers: harmless and shy, bolting from any sign of people, a mainstay of hunters' pots.

name: Vessino Clams
description: The Vessino Clam is a rare mollusk which is found only in the deep waters of the Haragaer and the southern Belegaer. A source of a valuable ingredient for a healing balm that soothes and heals burns, it is prized by Lay Healers and Medicinists. Its beautiful, silvery shell is also noted as an exquisite ornament.
description_summary: A rare deep-water clam of the southern seas, prized for an ingredient in a healing balm that soothes burns, and for its beautiful silvery shell. Toward travellers: harmless; a valued find for healers and a thing of beauty.

name: Sea Crocodiles
description: Uncommon but well-feared, the huge Sea Crocodiles resemble Fell Beasts without wings. They are gracile, dark blue, and have black stripes running down the backs of their horny backs. Many chroniclers believe they are related to Water Drakes, for Sea Crocodiles have elongated conical grasping teeth in the forward part of their snouts that extend well outside their mouths. Like sea monsters, Sea Crocodiles are prone to attack smaller ships, seeking to puncture hulls with their mighty jaws, then waiting for the ship to founder. Nesting in secluded bays or in coastal caves, they also present a danger on land.
description_summary: Huge, feared sea-reptiles — dark blue with black-striped backs and long grasping fangs that jut from the snout, thought kin to water-drakes — that attack small ships, punching holes in hulls and waiting for them to founder. Toward travellers: a deadly menace at sea, a wrecker of boats and devourer of those it spills into the water.

name: Devevi
description: The Devevi (Ap. "Camels"; sing. "Deve") of southern and central Endor are hardy animals who store water in their stomach linings and can go long periods without drinking. Their humps absorb heat and metabolize fat to provide energy and water, while wide foot pads enable them to walk on soft, sandy soil; a three-chambered stomach permits digestion of virtually any vegetation. With a refined temperature regulation system, a Deve has no need to sweat; it can lose over a quarter of its body weight and restore it in an orgy of drinking lasting only ten minutes (intaking 7-8 gallons a minute). There are two subgroups: the runners (light, graceful riding mounts built for speed) and the workers (thickly-built, slow, capable of heavy loads). Both are easy to domesticate but are loud, filthy, spit, and are virtually impossible to keep clean. The most prized is the rare two-humped Deve Póa, bred in and around the desert city of Tûl Póac in Far Harad and hailing from the Yellow Mountains of southern Endor; adapted for both temperature extremes, more docile, with a natural saddle and a smooth, rolling gait.
description_summary: Camels of the southern lands — hardy desert beasts that store water and fat, walk soft sand on broad pads, go long without drinking and endure fierce heat without sweating. Toward travellers: stolid, ill-tempered but invaluable desert mounts and pack-beasts, harmless unless provoked.

name: Glutani
description: Dreadful wolverine-like predator (pl. Glutani), solitary, dark-furred and badger-shaped, found throughout northwestern Endor with its greatest concentration in western Minhiriath. Unbelievably quick, fierce and seemingly insane, it attacks armed groups without provocation and fights to the death. Two varieties: the reddish-brown Lowland Glutan (~2 ft) and the bulkier, dark Highland Glutan of the Dunland hills. A 25 gp bounty sits on their heads; Dunlanders buy live ones for fights against Bears or Wolves.
description_summary: A dreadful wolverine-like predator — solitary, dark-furred and badger-shaped — unbelievably quick, fierce and seemingly mad, attacking even armed groups unprovoked and fighting to the death. Toward travellers: wildly aggressive for its size; it may set upon a whole party without cause, a danger out of all proportion to its bulk.

name: Cherethrynd
description: Powerful Sperm Whales, the Cherethrynd (S. "Hallmaw"; sing. "Charothrond") reside in the deep waters of the Belegaer, off the western coasts of Middle-earth. Unlike most Whales, they sport teeth (nine inch long teeth) and they hunt fish and Squid rather than consuming krill through a baleen strainer. The Charothrond favorite food and most dangerous rival is the Giant Squid. Although wily and capable of upsetting a good-sized vessel, Cherethrynd are vulnerable to the ruthless Umbarean whalers who sail the oceans near the Bay of Belfalas. These Men hunt lone Whales for their oil, bone, and ambergris (an indigestible chalky substance that forms around Squid beaks and, in spite of its hideous smell, makes wonderful perfumes).
description_summary: Powerful toothed sperm-whales of the western deeps that hunt fish and squid — their great rival the giant squid — and are wily enough to upset a good-sized vessel. Toward travellers: no hunter of men, but its sheer size can capsize a ship that crowds it.

name: Andodailion
description: The Andodaio, or "Gate-shadow" (pl. "Andodailion"), is a black, nocturnal lizard with a prehensile tongue and tail. It is without doubt the greatest climber among all the Rávatsar. Rugged landscapes and subterranean settings accommodate it well, for the Andodaio is insulated with an unusual array of three layers of hollow scales that enable it to survive in environments far too cool for its brethren. Hanging from rocks, it swings across the caves and precipices, leaping onto prey and tearing its victims apart with its steely claws. Larger creatures would have little to fear from the Andodailion, except that they live in large communities and often hunt in groups of 10-100 individuals. Unusually social, a horde of Andodailion can easily fell a Man by attacking in concert.
description_summary: A black, nocturnal lizard with a prehensile tongue and tail and three layers of hollow insulating scales — the greatest climber of its kind, swinging across caves and cliffs to leap on prey and rend it with steely claws. Toward travellers: a clinging cave-and-cliff predator; small, but its claws and ambush make dark rock dangerous.

name: Wild Horses
description: The Wild Horse, or Horse of the Plains (S. "Rechelaid"), make good mounts, although they are hard to break. Much used by Easterlings, they are now herded in Calenardhon (Rohan). A few may be found in the marshes, for they do not mind swimming.
description_summary: The wild horses of the plains — good mounts but hard to break, much used by Easterlings and now herded in Rohan; some live in the marshes, untroubled by swimming. Toward travellers: skittish free-roaming horses, harmless, and prized if one can catch and tame them.

name: Arthedain Site
description: A ruin or relic of Arthedain, northern kingdom of the Dúnedain — a fallen watchtower, road-marker or hall crumbling on the downs of old Arnor. Some still hold lore, treasure or older guardians.
description_summary: A ruin or relic of Arthedain, the lost northern Dúnedain kingdom — a fallen watchtower, road-marker or hall crumbling on the downs of old Arnor; some still hold lore, treasure or older guardians. Toward travellers: a melancholy landmark and possible shelter, though what lingers in such places is not always friendly.

name: Dunlendings Healers
description: Wise-women and herb-lore keepers of the Dunlending clans, tending wounds and sickness with simples, charms and old craft. Respected and a little feared among their own folk, they guard remedies and rites handed down since before Rohan came.
description_summary: Wise-women and herb-keepers of the Dunlending clans, tending wounds and sickness with simples, charms and old craft — respected and a little feared, guarding remedies and rites older than Rohan. Toward travellers: a possible source of healing and lore, though wary of outsiders and protective of their secrets.

name: Grey Apes
description: Grey, shaggy-haired, heavily-built apes (P. "Cijuvu Niavi", S. "Peredrugwath") that live alone, in pairs or in small family groups in the forests of Pel in southwestern Middle-earth. They stand up to 5 ft tall and weigh up to 300 lbs; long arms reach the ankles when erect, and broad opposable-digited hands plus great upper-body strength let them swing tree to tree with ease. Their short, weak legs make for an awkward, halting gait on the jungle floor, whether upright or on all fours.
description_summary: Big, shaggy grey apes of the southwestern forests — up to five feet and three hundred pounds, with long arms and powerful hands for swinging through trees but short, weak legs that make them awkward on the ground. Toward travellers: keep to the canopy and shun people; powerful and dangerous if threatened, but no hunter of men.

name: Coireals
description: This inoffensive snake inhabits forests and heavy copses; it can be readily identified by the bright yellow bands on its glossy black body. It is generally docile and will avoid people. Most bites occur when witless folk try to pick up this "pretty snake." Instead, the Coireal is happy to hunt large insects. It should be left alone, for its venom is highly potent (10th lvl), one of the most lethal natural poisons in Middle-earth. Symptoms appear in one to four hours, resulting in all but certain death from paralysis.
description_summary: A glossy black snake banded bright yellow, of forests and copses — docile and shy, hunting large insects and avoiding people; but its venom is among the most lethal in Middle-earth. Toward travellers: deadly only to the fool who handles the "pretty snake" — leave it be and it leaves you be.

name: Ash Drake
description: An ash/volcanic variety of Fire-drake, at home amid cinder and lava fields.
description_summary: A volcanic kind of fire-drake at home among cinder, ash and lava-fields. Toward travellers: a fire-dragon of the burning wastes, deadly and camouflaged amid the smoke and stone of its lairs.

name: Water Spring
description: A spring where water rises from the ground — a welcome find for the thirsty, though some run foul, mineral-tainted or scalding, and a few are held sacred or guarded by what dwells near them.
description_summary: A spring where water rises from the ground — a welcome find for the thirsty, though some run foul, mineral-tainted or scalding, and a few are held sacred or guarded by what dwells near. Toward travellers: usually a blessing on the road, but taste with care, and beware what may watch over it.

name: North Bears
description: Huge, pale-grey or white bears (Q. Megliformen) found around the Ice Bay of Forochel. Usually solitary, with an excellent sense of smell and superb swimming, they subsist mainly on seal and fish and range across well-defined coastal territories of about a hundred square miles, smelling intruders up to two miles off. In the brief northern summer they wander beyond their range to find a mate and gorge on roots and berries.
description_summary: Huge pale-grey or white bears of the icy northern bay — solitary, superb swimmers with a keen nose that scents intruders from miles off, living mostly on seal and fish across wide coastal territories. Toward travellers: avoid people, but immense and dangerous if met — and they know you are coming long before you see them.

name: Cliff
description: A sheer drop or precipice where a single misstep means a fatal fall. Cliffs bar the way along coasts, gorges and mountain paths, and a crumbling edge or sudden gust can send the careless over.
description_summary: A sheer drop or precipice where a single misstep means a fatal fall, barring the way along coasts, gorges and mountain paths. Toward travellers: a real and silent danger — a crumbling edge or sudden gust can send the careless over.

name: Great Falcons Of Ardor
description: These fierce creatures were the preferred mounts of the Lords of the Air of the Court of Ardor in southern Middle-earth. They were perhaps developed from the Great Falcons of Mirkwood. This fact would have been known to Sauron, who could have shared his knowledge with the evil Elda Court. The fiercest of these Falcons were tied to specific Windlords by magical means; their loyalty to their masters was unswerving, and no one else could ride them unless they could somehow overcome the will of the Falcons by magical means.
description_summary: Fierce giant falcons bred as mounts for evil Air-lords of the far south — perhaps developed from the great falcons of Mirkwood — the fiercest magically bound to a single master that none else can ride. Toward travellers: war-mounts of dark lords, a deadly aerial threat where their riders hold sway.

name: Black Minks
description: Voracious freshwater predator, much larger than common mink (50-80 lbs). Most common in the Wetwang (Nindalf) near Ithilien, otherwise in north-central Middle-earth always near water. Hunts alone but mobs in early-spring mating gangs of 7-12, attacking anything in its path. Its thick, glossy, waterproof pelt fetches 6-15 gp.
description_summary: A voracious giant freshwater mink (50-80 lbs) of marshes and waterways — usually a lone hunter, but in spring it gangs into mating-mobs that attack anything in their path. Toward travellers: a savage water-weasel; little threat singly, but a spring mob is dangerous near the water.

name: Harfoots (Common Hobbits)
description: The most common Hobbits are Harfoots. They are the smallest in size and the darkest in color, rarely exceeding 3 feet in height and having brown skin and hair. Neat and uniformly beardless, they are in many ways the picture of the average Hobbit. Harfoots favor hillsides and highlands. They have traditional sites for their traditional smials or Hobbit-hole houses. They prefer to interact with the grim Dwarves. All Hobbits share in hating Wargs and Orcs. They are the stay-at-home, hole-dwelling folk of the Shire, Bree and the old Hobbit-lands of Eriador, and are seldom met far afield.
description_summary: The commonest and smallest hobbits — brown-skinned, beardless, rarely over three feet — hole-dwelling, home-loving folk of hill and highland, the very picture of the ordinary Shire-hobbit, and friendly with Dwarves. Toward travellers: peaceable, shy, hospitable little folk who keep to themselves and hate orcs and wargs.

name: Stoors (River Hobbits)
description: The Stoors are the stockiest Hobbit folk and often appear shorter than some of the Harfoots because of their wide profile. Generally, however, they are taller than Harfoots and shorter than Fallohides. Their coloration is also somewhat of a compromise, although they have curly brown hair which resembles that of their shorter cousins. Nonetheless, the most distinguishing features of Stoors are their huge hands and feet (large even by Hobbit standards) and frequent beard growth. Stoors prefer riversides and flatlands. Some have begun relying on surface dwellings of wood, brick, or stone. Stoors trace their lines through the female. River- and marsh-loving folk, they are met poling boats and tending weirs in Dunland, the wetlands of Eriador and the Gladden country, and are warier of strangers than other Hobbits.
description_summary: The stockiest hobbits, broad-built with unusually large hands and feet and sometimes a beard — river- and flatland-dwellers, more at home near water than other hobbit-kinds. Toward travellers: easy-going, sturdy riverside folk, cautious with strangers but not unfriendly.

name: Fallohides (Forest Hobbits)
description: The Fallohides are the tallest and average between 3 and 4 feet in height. They have fair skin and, of all the groups, resemble Men the most. Their numbers are small, however, and some observers have confused them with nearby mannish groups. Fallohides enjoy the woodlands found in cool northern climes. Some have begun relying on surface dwellings of wood, brick, or stone. Fallohides are the most prone to mingle with Big People. Harfoots and Fallohides trace their lines through either the male or female. Those Hobbits who dare venture away from home are assumed to be extraordinary, and therefore get five background points.
description_summary: The tallest, fairest and fewest hobbits, the most Man-like and the boldest — woodland folk of the cool north, the readiest of hobbits to mingle with the Big People and to wander. Toward travellers: adventurous and relatively open for hobbits, friendly and curious about outsiders.

name: Black Númenoreans
description: The term "Black Númenorean" (S. "Mornúmenedain") is used to describe Men of Dúnedain stock who are descended from the "Unfaithful" Númenorean colonists and conquerers who came to Middle-earth during the middle and late Second Age. These Unfaithful had broken with the Valar and Elves who had bequeathed them the island kingdom of Númenor at the beginning of the Second Age, and many worshipped their own idols or paid homage to the Dark Lord Sauron. Physical Character: Strong and imposing, with angular features. Men average 225 pounds, women 150 pounds. After long years in the hot South, the Black Númenoreans have tanned, grey-brown skin, and black hair. Their piercing eyes are various shades of grey. Considerable endurance. Men average 6'4", women 5'10". Lifespan: 90-175 years. +25 to Sailing maneuvers. +10 to melee Offensive Bonuses, -10 to Defensive Bonus. Black and gold are their favored colors. They wear rich clothing made of dyed silk and cotton. Their societies are well-ordered and ruled by force of personality, backed by harsh law. Dictators and oligarchs hold sway over the people.
description_summary: Strong, imposing Men of Dúnedain blood descended from the Númenoreans who turned from the Valar to dark idols and the service of Sauron — proud, long-lived and steeped in old enmity for the Faithful. Toward travellers: arrogant and dangerous enemies of the free peoples, often allied with the Shadow.

name: Woses
description: Of all Men, none surpass the Woses in the arts of wood-lore and wood-craft. This very ancient and diminutive race has long been tied to the forests and has remained the greatest lot of woodland warriors ever produced by the Second-born. Their skills have in fact guarded their narrow survival, for they are considered ugly by Men and Elves alike and have been hunted and persecuted since the days of the First Age. The Woses have many names. What they call themselves is not altogether clear, although the name Drûgs is taken from their own tongue. Drúedain (sing. Drúadan) is the label given them by the Elves, while Men call them by various labels: Rógin (sing. "Róg"), Púkelmen, Wild Men, or Woses, the latter a Westron term. Orcs fear the Woses, and have named them Oghor-hai. Physical Character: Woses generally have a broad, stumpy profile marked by wide faces, flat features, and deep-set eyes. Short, thick legs, heavy lower bodies, and pronounced brows help give them an eerie character. Ruddy skin, black hair, and black eyes. 4 to 5 feet. Lifespan: 30-50 years. The wide noses of the Woses are the most sensitive found in Man. Even in an open field, they can smell an Orc before another Man can see him. +25 bonus to Tracking maneuvers; +15 bonus for Foraging. They have the ability to sit for days on end without movement. Woses are blessed with superb night-vision. Even in the dark forest of the night they can see 1000 feet as if it were daylight.

description_summary: The Drúedain — an ancient, diminutive forest-people unmatched in wood-craft and woodland warfare, thought ugly and long persecuted by Men and Elves, surviving hidden in the deep woods. Toward travellers: deeply wary and secretive after ages of hunting; no aggressors, but they watch unseen and trust few — yet stalwart friends to those who earn it.

name: Easterlings
description: This term refers to a collection of peoples who live in north-central Middle-earth, the area to the east of the western shore of the Sea of Rhûn. These nomadic confederations are ever in search of territory and wealth and periodically invade the lands of their neighbors. The Balchoth and Wainriders are two such peoples. Each group has its own subculture and language, although most are related. Physical Character: Compact and agile. Men average 140 pounds, women 100 pounds. Swarthy, with a tan or yellowish/tan quality. Normal endurance, although they can ride horses, wagons, or chariots for long periods without exhaustion. Short to medium. Men average 5'6", women 5'1". Lifespan: Short, about 40-65 years. They are superb with riding animals and produce fabulous horsemen. +10 bonus to Offensive Bonuses when fighting from a horse or chariot. +20 to any attempt to train, befriend, or subdue a horse. They are nomads who live in mobile camps and move their camps using great wagons (wains). Warriors all, they also herd horses and cattle. Easterlings have little regard for anyone except their own people. They hate the Dúnedain.
